text: Near Infrared In-situ Measurements for the Detection of Keyhole Porosity in Autogenous Ti-6Al-4V Welds ABSTRACT Certification of additive manufactured metal parts requires nondestructive evaluation (NDE) to ensure build quality. NDE can be performed during the build process or post build. For large parts with complex geometries, post build NDE can be challenging. In-situ NDE potentially provides a way to perform the inspection layer by layer. This work explores the use of a high speed near infrared (NIR) camera that is focused in-line with a laser to obtain high spatial and temporal resolution thermal imagery of the melt pool and associated cooling areas. The thermal data is obtained during a laser melting process using a Ti-6Al-4V plate and of particular interest is the detection of keyhole porosity. Keyhole porosity can result from non-optimal build conditions, such as excessive laser power at a given laser scanning speed, that creates an entrapped bubble. The NIR measured melt pool and cooling areas are processed to detect keyhole porosity. The results are compared to X-ray computed tomography (CT) for validation. Keyhole pores buried deep were not detectable with this technique, however, some larger subsurface elongated pores and some open surface pores did show some promise for detectability. INTRODUCTION Additively manufactured (AM) metal parts, built using laser powder bed fusion, is growing in aerospace applications. The advantages are that complex shapes and intricate internal geometries can be rapidly built. If these structures are to be used in primary load applications, they must be certified. The certification process will involve build quality assessment using nondestructive evaluation (NDE). NDE can be applied both during the build process (in-situ inspection) and post build (ex-situ inspection). Both inspection methodologies are required, however, in-situ inspection offers some advantages such as the potential to detect defects during the build layer by layer. If a significant defect is detected the build can be stopped to avoid wasting material and time. If repairable defects are detected, then processing measures can be employed to correct the defects and continue the build. In-situ NDE can therefore provide a capability that can reduce both build time and cost while ensuring improved part quality. Defects such as lack of fusion and keyhole porosity are understood to have a strong correlation with processing conditions. These defects can influence the mechanical performance of the AM built part.

These defects can influence the mechanical performance of the AM built part. In this work, we describe a Configurable Additive Testbed (CAT) system used for laser powder bed fusion AM. The CAT system is a custom developed AM system that is comprised of an enclosed environmental chamber which has been modified to conduct laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) experiments. The CAT is used to test various process parameters and materials and can be custom configured with a variety of sensors for in-situ NDE. We study the use of two high-speed, high-resolution cameras configured to image in the near infrared (NIR) band. The NIR cameras are positioned in-line optically with the laser, and therefore the camera field of view follows the laser. The NIR cameras allow for high resolution imaging of the melt pool and surround areas. The thermal data is obtained during a laser melting process on a Ti-6Al-4V plate and of particular interest is the detection of keyhole porosity. It is interesting to note the 7 – 7 nm band image had a significantly higher pixel intensity count as compared to the 8 – 9 nm NIR camera. This is most likely due to in-line optical path optics such as the mirrors, splitters, and focusing optics which suppressed the light in the longer waveband. The matrix An is normalized by dividing by the maximum value and offset corrected by subtracting the mean along the time dimension. The covariance matrixn is defined as the An TAn. The covariance matrixn is now a square matrix of number of images used for processing and can then be averaged for all the total number of data sets (N). The averaged covariance matrix can then be decomposed using singular value decomposition to calculate the fixed eigenvectors. The PCA inspection image is calculated by dot product multiplication of the selected fixed eigenvector times the temperature responses for each data set (data matrix An), pixel by pixel. Qualitatively some open pores or large pores buried near the surface (less than 2 microns) were detected, however, a thorough statistical analysis on more data sets will be required to establish feasibility and probability of detection since there were false indications as highlighted in. This technique perhaps may be more suited for lack of fusion porosity detection which can produce larger near surface pores. Also, efforts towards an automated data registration and comparison of the in-situ thermal measurements to the X-ray CT would be highly valuable since it is anticipated that processing of large data sets would be required for an AM build with many layers. Lastly, future investigation of modeling efforts will be necessary to further validate and optimize the inspection. Nevertheless, this inspection technique appears to have some potential for in-situ inspection during the AM building process.

Effect of process parameters on the mechanical behavior of Ti Al V alloys fabricated by laser powder bed fusion method The parameters used in the production process in laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF) technology, one of the additive manufacturing methods, have a critical effect on the mechanical behavior and density properties of the product and can change the properties of the product. Therefore, this study examined the influence of laser power, scanning speed, hatch spacing and beam rotation angle on the tensile strength, impact energy, surface roughness, and porosity levels of Ti–6Al–4V produced through the L-PBF method. It was the tensile strength changes directly proportional to the laser power. In addition, the tensile strength decreases significantly with a value of 6 MPa obtained at lowest 0.0 mm hatch spacing, 1 W laser power and 9 mm scanning speed. The lowest value of impact energy, with a value of 8.7 J, was reached at a rotation angle of 1 ◦. It has been determined that surface roughness decreases significantly with increasing laser power, while it demonstrates an increase with higher scanning speeds.

of impact energy, with a value of 8.7 J, was reached at a rotation angle of 1 ◦. It has been determined that surface roughness decreases significantly with increasing laser power, while it demonstrates an increase with higher scanning speeds. Surface roughness values of 8.9 μm parallel and 8.3 μm perpendicular to the building direction were achieved at a laser power level of 2 W. Laser power was identified as the most influential factor in determining the porosity ratio, with a high porosity rate 0.4 % occurring at 1 W laser power. Furthermore, an increase in scanning speed resulted in a corresponding increase in the porosity rate. Additive manufacturing (AM) technique based on the layer-by-layer production strategy was launched to be used for prototype production. AM has been started to be applied to meet the wide needs in automotive, aerospace, energy and medicine fields, as it allows the production of metal parts as net shaped with high mechanical and metallurgical properties, almost fully of dense and complex structures. The laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF) process has garnered significant attention as an additive manufacturing technique due to numerous advantages over traditional manufacturing methods, including the ability to create complex shapes, reduce material consumption, and produce parts with excellent mechanical properties.

laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF) process has garnered significant attention as an additive manufacturing technique due to numerous advantages over traditional manufacturing methods, including the ability to create complex shapes, reduce material consumption, and produce parts with excellent mechanical properties. The main concept is based on a laser beam selectively melting a thin layer (typically 2 μm) of metallic powder and fusing it layer by layer to form a net-shaped part according to slice data of CAD model. The majority of research in the field of L-PBF focuses on the materials titanium, nickel and iron. Ti–6 A l–4V is among the most widely used titanium alloys at 5 % worldwide, in consideration of its encouraging ratio of strength to weight, excellent tensile properties at ambient temperature, resistance to fatigue and excellent crack propagation. Extensive research efforts have been dedicated to the qualification of parts fabricated using AM technologies, with a specific focus on with laser beam energy for the titanium alloy Ti–6Al–4V. The L-PBF process employs various scanning strategies and powder bed preheating operations to generate different cooling rates and heat treatments. These strategies can reduce the generation of residual stress and increase yield strength and ductility by decomposing the martensitic microstructure into the equilibrium α+β microstructure.

In L-PBF process, laser power, scanning speed, layer thickness, and hatch spacing are common process parameters that greatly affect manufactured parts mechanical and metallurgical properties. Numerous studies have been carried out to explore how different parameters can affect the microstructure, process defects, and residual stresses in various materials . Several material properties, such as hardness, impact resistance, yield strength, ductility, and porosity level highly related with process parameters was examined. After conducting studies to determine the most influential parameters, researchers developed a volumetric energy density (EV) formulated to describe the relationship between laser power (P), scanning speed (v), hatch spacing (h), and layer thickness (t). Moreover, the EV parameter has often been utilized as a way of establishing a process range for producing fully dense components. The input of EV significantly impacts the microstructural features and mechanical properties of Ti–6Al–4V samples as they are deposited during the L-PBF process. Higher energy values suggest a combination of processing parameters resulting in excessive energy input into the powder bed, potentially causing the keyhole phenomenon and void formation. Conversely, lower E₀ values indicate insufficient energy input, which may lead to lack-of-fusion defects and poor layer welding. In accordance with the expressions, it is crucial to thoroughly examine the parameter combinations contributing to the energy value and achieve a well-balanced energy value. Studies in the literature have shown that maximum relative densities are obtained at different energy levels in Ti–6Al–4V components.

Studies in the literature have shown that maximum relative densities are obtained at different energy levels in Ti–6Al–4V components. In these studies, high relative densities could be obtained at various energy densities obtained at different levels of basic process parameters such as laser power, scanning speed, scanning range and layer thickness. In addition to these basic parameters, indirect parameters were also found to affect the material density. However, the concept of energy density does not provide a clear distinction of the specific types of defects that can occur in parts produced by L-PBF process. Therefore, it is not advisable to rely solely on energy density as the sole criterion for predicting defects. For example, increasing laser power or decreasing layer thickness may not fully compensate for the higher scanning speed and the combined effect on energy density may be different than expected. Therefore, a more comprehensive comparison of the parameters is necessary to fully assess their impact on the result. As a result of the literature review, it was seen that the L-PBF method is an increasingly important AM method and titanium alloys are produced by this method and used in many different industrial fields.

As a result of the literature review, it was seen that the L-PBF method is an increasingly important AM method and titanium alloys are produced by this method and used in many different industrial fields. Since the mechanical and metallurgical properties of the samples produced by this method are directly related to the process parameters used, it is critical to investigate their effects. Although there are many studies on this subject in the literature, the change of properties and the formation of defects have been investigated through the energy density parameter. However, since energy density is determined by more than one parameter, investigating the effects of individual parameters would be an important contribution to the literature. Therefore, in this study, the individual effects of laser power, scanning speed, hatch spacing and beam rotation angle on the tensile strength, impact energy, surface roughness and porosity levels of Ti–6Al–4V produced using the L-PBF process were investigated. Unlike the literature, different beam scanning strategies such as 4 ◦, 6 ◦ and 1 ◦ and different parameter combinations were used to evaluate mechanical properties including Charpy impact, tensile strength and surface roughness tests. Different surface measurement parameters were examined to determine the surface roughness of the produced samples. 2.

The expected increase in strength with higher laser power levels can be attributed to the inherent connection between energy input and resulting material characteristics, wherein greater energy input leads to reduced porosity, larger martensitic grain dimensions, and ultimately, enhanced strength properties. The changes of strengths depending on scanning speed are . While the laser power was kept constant at 1 W, the tensile strength decreased from 1 MPa at 8 mm/s (case V ) to 9 MPa at 1 mm/s (case V ) due to increasing scanning speed. This is a consequence of the reduction in energy density that takes place as the scanning speed is increased. It is available in the literature that high scanning speeds cannot guarantee sufficient a time for the entrapped gases to be released into the surroundings. Increased porosity levels typically result in a reduction in tensile strength, as the internal voids can serve as potential locations for stress concentration and structural failure. The reason for this decrease in impact energy is explained as; by the formation of martensitic (acicular α′ martensite on a columnar β grain) that exhibits a fine acicular (needle-like) microstructure due to the natural rapid cooling of the material. The presence of a fine and fully α′-martensite structure in as-built L-PBF samples of Ti–6Al–4V is a consequence of rapid cooling and results in high tensile strength a low ductility. Studies indicate that wrought Ti–6Al–4V alloys characterized by high α′ phase fractions exhibit significantly lower ductility when compared to their counterparts with both α and β phases. The reduction in ductility is likely a consequence of an increased proportion of the α′ phase, attributed to the accelerated heating and cooling along the beam path associated with higher scan speed. .3 J was attained, whereas at a speed of 1 mm/s (case V ), it escalated to 1 .7 J, reflecting a 1 % increase. Defects may occur due to insufficient solidification time or heat distribution due to the angle of rotation. During the keyhole mode, gas bubbles may surface at the base of the melt pool upon keyhole collapse. If the solidification time proves inadequate for the gas bubble to ascend to the surface, it results in the formation of a spherical pore within the solidified material. It has been increasing hatch spacing values resulted in a decrease in surface roughness for measurements taken in both directions. The hatch spacing, which is associated with the laser beam shaping strategy and determines the distance between the center lines of consecutive two laser scans, establishes the overlapping ratio of adjacent tracks and serves as a fundamental unit in additive manufacturing. Due to the high overlapping ratio, heat accumulation occurs. Thus, the interaction of the melt in the pool is strong and prolonged, and the splashing melt formed due to the large overlapping area reveals the convex surface. The presence of this convex surface can impact the application of the subsequent layer of powder during paving.

Title: Effect of powder characteristics on layer density, defects, and tensile properties of Ti-6Al-4V via laser powder bed fusion: establishing benchmark parameters for quality Feedstock powder characteristics affect the quality of metal parts manufactured by laser-based powder bed fusion of metals (PBFLB/M). However, identifying the decisive powder characteristics remains challenging due to the complexity of the PBF-LB/M process and lack of in-situ, process-relevant data. This study assesses four gas-atomised (GA) virgin Ti-6Al-4V powders using an SLM Solutions® system. The surface chemistry, morphological constituents, particle size distribution (PSD) and rheological properties of each powder are investigated. Furthermore, using a powder-bed capsule approach, combined with micro-computed tomography (μCT) analyses, the powder-bed layer density (ρlayer) averaged over 1 layers in real PBF-LB/M is determined for respective powders and correlated to the defects and tensile properties of the as-printed Ti-6Al-4V specimens. We demonstrate that ρlayer is critical to the as-printed quality and propose a benchmark ρlayer of at least 6 % of the theoretical density of Ti-6Al-4V to achieve highquality builds. Additionally, it was none of the conventional powder morphological and rheological properties, such as apparent density, tap density, and angle of repose, are sufficiently informative as decisive powder characteristics for predicting PBFLB/M quality.

density of Ti-6Al-4V to achieve highquality builds. Additionally, it was none of the conventional powder morphological and rheological properties, such as apparent density, tap density, and angle of repose, are sufficiently informative as decisive powder characteristics for predicting PBFLB/M quality. We further propose that, within the specified powder size range, the powder parameters D (a particle surface area-based mean diameter) and D (a particle volume-based mean diameter) should be tailored to the stabilised layer thickness of the selected PBF-LB/M process as supplementary benchmark parameters. This layer thickness can be calculated from the nominal layer thickness and ρlayer using an analytical model. The proposed powder-bed capsule-μCT approach can serve as a unique powder assessment method for PBF-LB/M. This study also investigates the role of fine powder particles within the PSD and their impact on packing density, revealing that an optimal balance of fine particles is crucial for enhancing powder-bed layer density while mitigating the risks associated with agglomeration and instability. Laser-based powder bed fusion of metals (PBF-LB/M) is an established additive manufacturing (AM) process that uses fine spherical powders (1 μm) as feedstock. Their characteristics directly influence the surface and internal quality of the printed parts. However, it remains challenging to explicitly define a single or a set of parameters for powder selection because of the complexity of the PBF-LB/M process.

as feedstock. Their characteristics directly influence the surface and internal quality of the printed parts. However, it remains challenging to explicitly define a single or a set of parameters for powder selection because of the complexity of the PBF-LB/M process. As a result, the connection between powder characteristics and part quality is yet to be fully established. In fact, inconsistencies are common in published experimental data or observations regarding the effect of the powder characteristics on build quality. Powder-bed layer density (ρlayer) is a critical parameter that affects the quality of the printed parts. Jacob and co-workers first developed a powder bed capsule approach to measuring the ρlayer for PBF-LB/M. The approach is based on printing a cylindrical capsule that can capture the metal powder from each layer of spreading during the PBF-LB/M process (see subsequent ). After printing, the powder in the capsule is weighed to determine the average powder layer density according to the number of layers that have been spread. A similar approach was developed by, who printed open cubic containers (1 ×1 ×1 mm ) to identify the average powder-bed layer density during PBF-LB/M. Compared with conventional powder characterization methods, the powder-bed capsule approach offers in-situ powder-bed layer density data from a PBF-LB/M process and is therefore more relevant.

containers (1 ×1 ×1 mm ) to identify the average powder-bed layer density during PBF-LB/M. Compared with conventional powder characterization methods, the powder-bed capsule approach offers in-situ powder-bed layer density data from a PBF-LB/M process and is therefore more relevant. However, to date, experimental studies on the influence of the powder-bed layer density on part quality and properties remain scarce as this is a less straightforward characterization method than conventional methods. Various methods have been employed for this purpose, each tailored to specific objectives. Some techniques involve scooping random samples from the bed, while others employ targeted extraction devices or powder capsules. These approaches differ in accuracy and flexibility, as described by in their recent study. Powder capsules, specialized structures that encapsulate powder, offer precise powder collection from specific zones and layers, yielding highly accurate results. Apart from the particle size distribution (PSD), various other powder characteristics and non-powder-related variables affect the ρlayer and the PBF-LB/M process. These include particle shape, particle surface roughness, particle surface chemistry (oxidation or contamination, which affects particle cohesion and agglomeration ), powder bed temperature, circulating gas and powder spreading or recoating mechanism . It is therefore not surprising that quantifying the effect of powder characteristics on build quality remains challenging.

chemistry (oxidation or contamination, which affects particle cohesion and agglomeration ), powder bed temperature, circulating gas and powder spreading or recoating mechanism . It is therefore not surprising that quantifying the effect of powder characteristics on build quality remains challenging. Moreover, the powder layer density heavily depends on the dispersal mechanism of the PBF-LB/M system. This study adds to the quest for the decisive powder characteristics for PBF-LB/M. The first part focuses on the conventional characterization of four gas-atomised (GA) Ti-6Al-4V powders, including their morphological constituents, PSD, rheological properties, and surface condition using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). This is followed by using the powderbed capsule approach, assisted with micro-computed tomography (μCT), to quantify the actual powder-bed layer density. Then we examine the correlation of each quantified powder characteristic with the defects and tensile properties of the as-printed samples. Finally, we conclude the work with a range of insightful observations for powder selection for PBF-LB/M. This study investigates the impact of powder characteristics on Ti-6Al-4V part quality in PBF-LB/M. It establishes a correlation between the powder-bed layer density anddefects, tensile properties, highlighting its importance. Furthermore, it proposes that, within the specified powder size range, optimizing particle parameters D andD based on stabilised layer thickness is essential for enhanced PBF-LB/M outcomes. In the area of PBF-LB/M, with a particular focus on the powder recycling process, a pivotal consideration lies in the reduction of fine particles within a PSD, leading to a consequent decline in the Xfines value to less than 0.3.

In the area of PBF-LB/M, with a particular focus on the powder recycling process, a pivotal consideration lies in the reduction of fine particles within a PSD, leading to a consequent decline in the Xfines value to less than 0.3. This unavoidable phenomenon results in a cascade of noteworthy effects, especially within the context of powder PSD. First and foremost, when Xfines falls below 0.3, it signifies that a relatively modest fraction of fine particles is sufficient to achieve the desired PBF-LB/M optimal packing density. In this scenario, coarse particles assume a prominent role in efficient packing due to their superior flowability, contributing to the overall stability of the powder bed. Furthermore, an Xfines value below 0.3 helps the packing density become notably less susceptible to fluctuations in fine particle size or concentration, especially during the recycling of powder. This reduced sensitivity proves to be of immense practical value in PBF-LB/M processes, alleviating the need for exact fine particle control in dictating PBFLB/M procedures. Moreover, achieving optimal packing density with a minimal proportion of fine particles emerges as a compelling cost-saving measure. Beyond economic benefits, the risk of agglomeration or clumping, a potential pitfall associated with an abundance of fine particles, is significantly diminished.

The observed defects in the printed samples can be directly linked to specific powder characteristics and processing parameters, as evidenced by the results. The defect analysis reveals that elongated pores are primarily located near the surfaces, which aligns with the density and microstructural data (Figs. 1 and 1 ). These elongated pores are likely a consequence of insufficient powder spreading, potentially influenced by variations in powder flowability and powderbed density. Notably, the 2 µm powder from Supplier B exhibited a lower powder-bed density (2.6 g/cm ) compared to Supplier A (2.9 g/cm ), despite Supplier B’s powder showing superior morphological sphericity and a narrower PSD span, as detailed in. This discrepancy suggests that additional factors, such as the recoating mechanism or inter-particle cohesion, may play a significant role in layer formation. Powder morphology appears to have a pronounced impact on defect formation. The higher proportion of elongated and agglomerated particles in Supplier A’s powder ( and ) is expected to enhance inter-particle packing during spreading.

When a suitable, reasonably wide PSD is present, the effective filling of voids can result in a denser powder bed, as observed in Supplier A’s powder. In contrast, the higher percentage of spherical particles in Supplier B’s powder does not lead to improved powder-bed density. Along with reduced interlocking between particles, the narrower PSD of Supplier B’s powder likely hinders the effective filling of voids, which may be the primary cause. The optical classification results and rheological measurements support this hypothesis, demonstrating better flowability for Supplier A’s powder despite its broader PSD. The analysis of surface chemistry and oxide film thickness reveals no significant differences between the powders from Suppliers A and B, eliminating these factors as potential contributors to the observed differences in defect formation. Similarly, the negligible internal porosity observed in both powders suggests that particle porosity is not a determining factor in the observed disparities. However, the µCT analysis shows that the primary cause of the increased defect density in samples produced using Supplier B’s powder is poor layer compaction, rather than keyhole porosity. The tensile properties and fractography further confirm the relationship between defect formation and powder characteristics. Samples fabricated using Supplier A’s powder exhibit superior tensile strength and ductility, which can be attributed to the lower defect density and improved powder-bed packing achieved during the AM process. Conversely, the increased occurrence of voids in samples produced with Supplier B’s powder highlights the impact of powder-bed density on mechanical performance. Although the powder from Supplier A may initially seem inferior based on conventional metrics such as sphericity and PSD span, it exhibits superior performance due to enhanced powder-bed packing and a lower defect density. These findings underscore the necessity of adopting a comprehensive approach when evaluating powder suitability for AM applications, considering a broader range of factors beyond standard metrics.

It provides an informative assessment of the flowability of the powder. Achieving an optimal ρlayer is critical for attaining the desired mechanical properties in PBF-LB/M. The PSD and the extent of compaction during powder spreading significantly influence effective powder packing on the powder bed. A low ρlayer can lead to irregularly shaped voids, porosity, and feeble particle bonding, resulting in diminished mechanical strength and ductility. This phenomenon is particularly pertinent to materials like Ti-6Al-4V, as underscored by the findings of this study. The ρlayer is intrinsically linked to the uniformity and extent of powder packing, which in turn profoundly impacts the selective melting process. All tensile samples in this study were printed in the vertical orientation to maintain consistency and minimise variability associated with different build directions, allowing for a clearer understanding of the influence of powder characteristics. However, this orientation, commonly used in PBF-LB/M studies, is known to influence mechanical properties due to the inherent anisotropy of the process, which arises from the columnar grain structure along the build direction.

However, this orientation, commonly used in PBF-LB/M studies, is known to influence mechanical properties due to the inherent anisotropy of the process, which arises from the columnar grain structure along the build direction. While this study primarily focuses on the effects of powder characteristics and defects, it is recognised that the vertical build orientation has also influenced the observed tensile properties, particularly in terms of ductility and strength. Recent detailed experiments have shown that following a hot isostatic pressing (HIP) treatment—applied to heal defects in Ti-6Al-4V samples and relieve residual stresses, thereby achieving an almost defect-free and stressfree state—the differences in tensile properties between vertically and horizontally built samples become negligible. Conversely, significant differences are evident prior to the HIP treatment, suggesting that the tensile property anisotropy of Ti-6Al-4V fabricated by PBF-LB/M is largely attributable to the presence of defects, which are influenced by the feedstock powder characteristics. However, other properties, such as fatigue performance, may still be influenced by the build direction even after the HIP treatment, for example, due to variations in grain boundary areas associated with different build directions. Notably, in these assessments, the residual stresses in the Ti-6Al-4V samples were inherently low due to the elevated powder bed temperatures used during fabrication.

Notably, in these assessments, the residual stresses in the Ti-6Al-4V samples were inherently low due to the elevated powder bed temperatures used during fabrication. Residual stresses in PBF-LB/M as-built Ti-6Al-4V components are influenced by a wide range of processing parameters and geometric factors, including powder bed density, build direction, scanning strategies, and part wall thickness. Powder bed density (3.0 vs. 2.6 g/cm for powders from Suppliers A and B, ) influences heat conductivity, which subsequently affects residual stresses. However, experimental data on this aspect remain scarce to date. Vertical builds tend to exhibit higher tensile residual stresses (up to 9 MPa) compared to horizontal builds (approximately 6 MPa) due to greater thermal gradients along the build direction. These stresses can lead to premature fracture and reduced ductility. Horizontal samples generally perform better under tensile loading than vertical samples. Surface defects and sharp notches in vertical samples act as stress concentrators, exacerbating crack initiation and promoting premature failures. Post-AM stress-relief techniques, such as heat treatment or machining, are effective in reducing residual stresses to below 1 MPa, significantly improving elongation and tensile strengths, particularly in vertical samples.

Post-AM stress-relief techniques, such as heat treatment or machining, are effective in reducing residual stresses to below 1 MPa, significantly improving elongation and tensile strengths, particularly in vertical samples. Nonetheless, a high ρlayer is expected to mitigate residual stresses, thereby benefiting the structural integrity of PBFLB/M as-built components. Finally, it should be emphasized that different PBF-LB/M systems use dissimilar powder spreading methods, which may override or intensify the difference in PSD between different feedstock powders. In that regard, the research findings of this study are restricted to SLM Solutions® systems, but they are expected to be informative for PBFLB/M with other systems. #The following key points about the influence of feedstock powder characteristics on PBF-LB/M of Ti-6Al-4V, using an SLM Solutions® system, can be drawn from this study. • The average powder-bed layer density can serve as a primary criterion for powder selection. For PBF-LB/M of Ti-6Al-4V (4.4 g cm−3) using SLM Solutions® systems, we propose a benchmark powder-bed layer relative density of at least 6 % for high-quality builds. # • The combined powder-bed-capsule and micro-CT approach provides an effective method for evaluating the actual powder-bed layer density of a given feedstock powder.

we propose a benchmark powder-bed layer relative density of at least 6 % for high-quality builds. # • The combined powder-bed-capsule and micro-CT approach provides an effective method for evaluating the actual powder-bed layer density of a given feedstock powder. Additionally, it offers a unique approach for assessing the powder-packing capability of the powderspreading system used in the selected PBF-LB/M system. • Within the specified nominal powder size range, we recommend that the selected powder have a broad PSD spanning from Dv(1 ) to Dv(9 ), with its D and D values—two supplementary benchmark parameters—tailored to match the stabilised layer thickness during PBF-LB/M. The stabilised layer thickness can be determined using. This PSD−D −D approach offers a simple alternative for powder assessment or selection. • None of the conventional powder morphological and rheological properties quantified in this study, including the percentage of each morphology, apparent density, tap density, their ratio, angle of repose, avalanche angle, and flowability assessment, were found to be sufficiently informative as decisive powder characteristic parameters for PBF-LB/M. Moreover, a higher fraction of highly spherical particles in the feedstock did not yield any noticeable benefit. These findings have broader implications beyond the specific PBF-LB/M system used in this study, suggesting that relying on traditional metrics for powder quality may not necessarily lead to improved outcomes in PBF-LB/M.

Our findings indicate that an optimal proportion of fine particles (approximately 3 wt.%) can significantly improve packing density without leading to agglomeration. A sufficient amount of fine powders should be retained during recycling to ensure consistent PBF-LB/M performance. • The research findings of this study are derived from PBF-LB/M of Ti-6Al-4V using an SLM Solutions® 1 system, but they are expected to be insightful for PBF-LB/M with other systems. Specifically, similar approaches to powder assessment, such as the use of powder-bed density benchmarks and tailored PSD parameters, may prove valuable for improving build quality in a wider range of AM applications.

Evaluation of residual stress in stainless steel 3 L and Ti Al V samples produced by selective laser melting Selective laser melting (SLM) has great potential in additive manufacturing because it enables the production of full-density complex parts with the desired inner structure and surface morphology. High temperature gradients as a result of the locally concentrated energy input lead to residual stresses, crack formation and part deformation during processing or after separation from the supports and the substrate. In this study, an X-ray diffraction technique and numerical simulation were used for investigating the residual stress in SLM samples fabricated from stainless steel 3 L and Ti Al Valloy. Conclusions regarding directions and values of stresses in SLM objects are given. High temperature gradients during selective laser melting (SLM) processing lead to high residual stresses. Cyclic rapid heating and cooling leads to residual stress up to the ultimate yield strength and not only causes deformation in parts and changes in geometric dimensions, but the formation of cracks and even delamination of the part from the substrate and the supports as well. The deformation occurs in various sections of an object due to different thermal gradients and temperature-dependent properties of the material.

SLM is an additive manufacturing technology which produces parts with a fusion of powder material track by track and layer by layer. The behaviour of individual SLM tracks and their geometric characteristics depend on the process parameters, and the physical–chemical and granulomorphometrical properties of the powder ( 2 ). The SLM object has properties of rapidly solidified material and features inherent in the complex structures, because the SLM object is a complex superposition of tracks and layers, and the properties of the object depend on the strength of ‘fixing’ the unit elements, which are tracks and layers. Due to the nature of manufacturing, SLM parts have the anisotropy of mechanical properties (Yadroitsev 2 ). The residual stress depends on the material properties, the geometry of the samples and the support structures, the process parameters as energy input, the powder layer thickness, the scanning strategy, preheating, etc. The analysis of the values and the directions of the stresses in SLM is a vital issue for providing reliable properties of high-quality SLM parts.

Impact of different pore types on the tensile and fatigue properties of AlSi Mg parts produced by laser powder bed fusion The mechanical properties of laser powder bed fusion (PBF-LB) parts such as fatigue strength or tensile strength are greatly influenced by the porosity occurring in the material. While it is common to achieve relative densities over 9 .5% for the aluminum alloy AlSi Mg, in practice, the formation of statistically occurring oxide pores cannot be avoided completely. Due to their irregular shape, these pores are detrimental for the mechanical properties. It is hypothesized that oxide pores can be avoided by increasing the energy input. This in turn leads to the formation of spherical keyhole pores. However, it is not sufficiently understood how keyhole porosity affects the mechanical properties with regard to oxide pores. Therefore, this work investigates the impact of keyhole pores, lack of fusion pores and oxide pores on the mechanical properties of PBF-LB AlSi Mg parts. Results show the importance of single critical defects for the fatigue behavior compared to the impact of relative porosity and reveal the less detrimental nature of keyhole pores compared to oxide pores.

the mechanical properties of PBF-LB AlSi Mg parts. Despite a much higher relative porosity, the tensile strength of specimens containing keyhole pores is comparable to that of highly dense parts whereas the fatigue strength has even improved Laser powder bed fusion (PBF-LB) is nowadays an established industrial process for manufacturing lightweight metal parts with complex shapes. While it is widely used for tool and medical applications, the use for automotive and aerospace applications is still hindered by the lack of technological knowledge of the behavior of load bearing PBF-LB parts. Mechanical properties of PBF-LB parts, especially fatigue behavior, differ significantly from cast or wrought parts due to typical process-related characteristics, such as rough surfaces, heterogeneous microstructure, and various pore defects. The aluminum alloy AlSi Mg is widely used for PBFLB parts in automotive and aerospace applications due to its high specific strength, good processability in PBF-LB, and low shrinkage. In recent years, numerous studies aimed to improve the understanding of the impact of AlSi Mg PBF-LB part characteristics on the mechanical properties. The current progress on this topic is well .

Nicoletto for example specifically investigated the influence of the surface finish on the fatigue properties. heat treatments can be beneficial for the fatigue strength and crack propagation characteristics of AlSi Mg PBF-LB parts. proved that experimental results of fatigue strength investigations of AlSi Mg PBF-LB parts are transferable to actual components. However, numerous studies conclude that pore defects have a major effect on the mechanical properties and in particular on the fatigue strength. showed in a recent experimental study that for a surface roughness Ra the effect of residual stress, surface roughness, and porosity on the fatigue life of AlSi Mg PBF-LB parts, proving porosity to be a dominant factor for the fatigue properties. Several studies have shown that for the PBF-LB process, a certain amount of porosity is inevitable. There are different types of pore defects that can occur in the PBF process.

porosity to be a dominant factor for the fatigue properties. Several studies have shown that for the PBF-LB process, a certain amount of porosity is inevitable. There are different types of pore defects that can occur in the PBF process. Lack of fusion (LoF) pores form either due to insufficient heat input and thus incomplete melting of the powder or due to defects in the spreaded powder layer. Keyhole (KH) pores often occur due to excessive heat input, e.g., at the end of scan vectors because of the dwell time of the laser beam. PBF-LB parts manufactured using the aluminum alloy AlSi Mg can additionally contain hydrogen pores and oxide pores. Hydrogen pores form by agglomeration of hydrogen atoms in the melt pool. Hydrogen is either captured during the powder atomizing process or stems from moisture gathered during powder handling. These pores are small spherically shaped and have no significant impact on the mechanical properties. During the PBF-LB process, the oxidation of aluminum with residual oxygen in the process chamber leads to the formation of an oxide layer on the melt surface and on previously solidified layers. Due to the high melting temperature of aluminum oxide, the oxide layer remains solid and acts as a separating layer which hinders the propagation of the melt pool.

an oxide layer on the melt surface and on previously solidified layers. Due to the high melting temperature of aluminum oxide, the oxide layer remains solid and acts as a separating layer which hinders the propagation of the melt pool. This leads to insufficient remelting of the previously solidified layers and adjacent scan tracks. As a result, cavities containing oxide layers remain between the melt pool, scan tracks, and previous layers. These oxide pores have an irregular shape and can reach several 1 µm in diameter. In numerous studies, oxide pores have been identified as critical (“killer”) defects during fatigue testing of AlSi Mg PBF-LB parts. Increased energy input during PBF-LB could break up the oxide layers to ensure sufficient bonding of the generated track to the previous layers but in turn gives rise to keyhole pore formation. Different models were proposed to assess the impact of pore defects on the fatigue life. proposed a method to measure the size of defects (√area) and calculate the respective stress intensity factors. The El Haddad approach uses the √area-value to estimate the fatigue limit. Experimental investigations with AlSi Mg show good agreement with the proposed models. Based on these results, Strauß et al. recently developed an equivalent stress model to analytically predict the fatigue life by including surface roughness and residual stress.

Based on these results, Strauß et al. recently developed an equivalent stress model to analytically predict the fatigue life by including surface roughness and residual stress. Moreover, numerical models were proposed to assess the impact of pores on the fatigue behavior. However, all these investigations were carried out using only specimens with high relative densities and a minimal porosity consisting of oxide and hydrogen pores and under the assumption that high relative densities yield the best mechanical properties. Further, it is unclear if the proposed models are still valid for larger amounts of porosity. In an experimental study with the material Ti Al V, the impact of different amounts of porosity on tensile and fatigue strength was investigated. The results LoF pores are much more harmful to the mechanical properties, whereas small amounts of KH only have a negligible impact. The analysis did not include the role of single critical defects, and moreover, oxide defects are not an issue when using titanium alloys in PBF-LB compared to aluminum alloys. The present work therefore investigates the impact of keyhole pores on the mechanical properties of AlSi Mg PBF-LB parts compared to the role of oxide defects and LoF defects.

In a first step, specimens were manufactured using different process parameters and the resulting porosity configurations were analyzed. Tensile and fatigue tests are conducted and the critical defects leading to the failure of the fatigue specimens were analyzed in detail to assess the influence of different pore morphologies on the fatigue strength. The paper aims to underline the importance of fatigue properties, the superior role of single critical defects, and their size and shape compared to the overall porosity, which is currently commonly used as the central quality criterion. The porosity of each parameter set was evaluated on a crosssectional area of one specimen of every pore configuration using at least three optical microscopy images per cross-section. Cross-sectional cuts were all prepared parallel to the build direction in the middle of the specimens. The porosity of the fatigue specimens was measured in the 1 cm at the top and bottom of the specimen and then averaged. Pores are classified as “near-surface” if the ratio of the radius of a circle with the same area as the critical defect divided by the distance of the center of the defect to the surface is>0.8. As all critical defects are close to or at the surface, this is true for all investigated defects. According to the presented criterium, none of the critical defects has to be classified as a pore cluster as the distance to adjacent pores in the fracture surface is sufficiently large. The fatigue limit defines the maximum stress level at which 1E load cycles (run out) or more can be performed without part failure. The fatigue limit of defect free specimens was estimated with 1.6 times the material hardness in HV. With hardness values of 1 HV for the HD specimens and 1 HV for the KH specimens, the estimated fatigue limit is 2 .4 MPa and 1 .8 MPa, respectively. The long crack (LC) theory considers the influence of defects and is determined by the material constant ∆Kth-LC, which is given with 3.2 MPa√m for AlSi Mg processed with PBF-LB. The intersection point with the fatigue limit gives the critical crack length a which is 4 .7 µm for HD specimens and 5 .4 µm for KH specimens. This could be subject of further research. As seen in, the fatigue behavior of PBF-LB parts is determined by one single defect, at which the crack starts to grow. This defect is typically at or near the surface. The analysis of the critical defects shows that the size and shape of KH pores lead to a smaller √area and, therefore, lower stress intensity factor and higher fatigue limit. This presumably leads to a delayed crack initiation for KH pores compared to oxide pores, which are the typical critical defect for HD specimens. Hence, the stress amplitude necessary to initiate a crack in the first place is also higher for KH pores resulting in the observed higher stress amplitude for the run-out specimen. This is supported by the slightly higher critical crack length a for KH specimens compared to HD specimens. The El Haddad model predicting the fatigue limit based on a single critical defect fits for a substantial amount of keyhole porosity. This indicates that for this porosity configuration, the single defect still defines the fatigue behavior.

When comparing the present results with other experimental studies with similar boundary conditions, it becomes clear that many more factors determine the performance during fatigue testing. In the present study, the fatigue strength (amplitude) of the standard high-density specimens was measured at 6 MPa with an average critical defect size of 3 µm. Strauß et al. also performed rotating bending fatigue tests which resulted in a fatigue strength of 1 MPa with a critical defect size of around 5 µm. Fini el al., however, only measured a fatigue strength of 4 MPa for 1E load cycles instead of 1E load cycles. reached a fatigue strength of 8 MPa with a critical defect size of around 1 µm. Nevertheless, the results clearly indicate that the fatigue behavior is significantly determined by single critical defects and their size. The modeling of the fatigue behavior using the LC theory heavily relies on the value of material constant ∆Kth-LC. The correct value, however, is not exactly known and is still disputed in the literature. While Strauß et al. use 3.2 MPa√m and achieved a good correlation with their experiments, they also proposed an adjusted value of 3.9 MPa√m. Other investigations recommend a value between 3.2 and 3.5 MPa√m or 3.6 MPa√m. For the present study, the LC theory using 3.2 MPa√m fits quite well with the experimental results. It is, however, clear that more investigations are needed to narrow down the correct value. The aim of the present work was to investigate the impact of different amounts of porosity and pore morphology on the tensile and fatigue strength of AlSi Mg parts produced by laser powder bed fusion. The main results are: • While keyhole pores only have a small impact on the tensile properties with an UTS of 3 MPa compared to 4 MPa for highly dense specimens, lack of fusion pores disproportionally decrease the tensile strength due to their irregular shape leading to high stress concentrations.

a small impact on the tensile properties with an UTS of 3 MPa compared to 4 MPa for highly dense specimens, lack of fusion pores disproportionally decrease the tensile strength due to their irregular shape leading to high stress concentrations. • The fatigue behavior of parts with keyhole pores is not determined by the porosity but by a single critical defect from which the crack propagates. • Highly dense AlSi Mg PBF-LB parts often contain irregularly shaped oxide pores acting as critical defects and causing premature crack initiation. • Due to the smaller size and spherical shape, keyhole pores are less likely to initiate a crack compared to oxide pores, and therefore, specimens with keyhole pores show an improved fatigue strength (8 MPa for 1E load cycles) compared to highly dense specimens with oxide pores (6 MPa for 1E load cycles). It is, therefore, advisable to manufacture load bearing aluminum parts with a slightly excessive energy input to avoid the formation of oxide pores. These findings are especially relevant for the manufacturing of safety relevant and dynamically stressed parts in the aviation sector such as brackets or bearing clampings.

These findings are especially relevant for the manufacturing of safety relevant and dynamically stressed parts in the aviation sector such as brackets or bearing clampings. Future work will be focused on optimizing the powder bed fusion process parameters to completely avoid the formation of oxide pores and simultaneously minimize the size of the more tolerable keyhole pores. Fatigue tests with specimens manufactured with optimized process parameters will demonstrate the potential of controlling the critical defect size when manufacturing aluminum parts using PBF-LB. Moreover, investigations on the influence of pores and porosity on crack initiation and crack propagation will be helpful for the assessment of defects in large parts.

Effects of process parameters on porosity in laser powder bed fusion revealed by X-ray tomography This paper reports on X-ray tomography of a series of coupon samples (5 mm cubes) produced under different process parameters, for laser powder bed fusion of Ti Al V. Different process parameters result in different pore formation mechanisms, each with characteristic pore sizes, shapes and locations within the 5 mm cube samples. While keyhole pores, lack of fusion pores and metallurgical pores have been previously identified and illustrated using X-ray tomography, this work extends beyond prior work to show how each of these not only exist in extreme situations but how they vary in size and shape in the transition regimes. It is shown how keyhole mode porosity increases gradually with increasing power, and how this depends on the scan speed. Similarly, lack of fusion pores are shown to occur following scan tracks in situations of poor hatch overlap, or a similar but different distribution of lack of fusion porosity due to large layer height spacing, showing respectively vertical and horizontal lack of fusion pore morphologies. Increased spacing between hatch scan tracks and contour scan tracks is demonstrated to form a near-surface porosity similar to that previously reported for slowing at the end of scan tracks which can cause keyhole mode porosity.

of fusion pore morphologies. Increased spacing between hatch scan tracks and contour scan tracks is demonstrated to form a near-surface porosity similar to that previously reported for slowing at the end of scan tracks which can cause keyhole mode porosity. Insights from 3D images allow improvements in parameter choices for optimized density of parts produced by laser powder bed fusion, and generally allow a better understanding of the porosity present in additively manufactured parts. Introduction Additive manufacturing (AM) of metals and particularly laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) has seen significant growth in recent years, with associated improvements in material quality, density and subsequently also improved mechanical properties – a recent comprehensive review of the technology is . Driven by applications in industries such as medical and aerospace, which require extremely high quality and reliable parts, there have been significant efforts at optimizing processes and ensuring superior mechanical properties, with great success. Especially the biomedical titanium alloy Ti Al V has been studied widely with reported excellent mechanical properties when processes have been optimized, see for example. With the development of AM and its wider adoption, there is an ever-increasing design complexity that is sought after for functional end-use parts. Therefore, quality control requirements increase in importance and becomes challenging due to the complexity.

see for example. With the development of AM and its wider adoption, there is an ever-increasing design complexity that is sought after for functional end-use parts. Therefore, quality control requirements increase in importance and becomes challenging due to the complexity. There is a long list of possible errors, flaws and imperfections in the LPBF manufacturing process, which can all lead to compromised mechanical properties. This has led to variability in the resulting mechanical properties reported, especially in earlier studies. There has been found especially large scatter in fatigue tests, and also differences in yield strength and elongation to failure in static tensile tests. These may be attributed to three main causes: unwanted residual stress, poor microstructure, or the presence of porosity or inclusions in the material. As quality control in AM improves and an improved understanding of the LPBF process develops, the role of porosity is increasingly recognized. The effects of excessive porosity has been shown to reduce mechanical properties, for example in a recent study of LPBF of Ti Al V as-built samples, it was shown how the tensile strength and elongation to failure varies with process parameters which create different forms of pores.

shown to reduce mechanical properties, for example in a recent study of LPBF of Ti Al V as-built samples, it was shown how the tensile strength and elongation to failure varies with process parameters which create different forms of pores. The role of pores, inclusions and surface defects on fatigue life of metals has been reviewed recently where it is shown that pores, especially those near the surface are critical and act as crack initiators. Quality control of porosity in parts by using X-ray tomography and/or metallurgical cross-sections is already widely adopted. Additionally, quality control at all levels of the process is critical, from powder feedstock, to process parameters, shielding gas, stress-relief heat treatment and other post-process treatments. In general, the optimal process parameters for LPBF of a specific material are best found by considering single track formation at a range of laser power densities and finding the best regime for stable single track formation – the basic building block of the process. This has been done with great success as described in.

by considering single track formation at a range of laser power densities and finding the best regime for stable single track formation – the basic building block of the process. This has been done with great success as described in. This is especially useful when developing new materials and makes it simple to separate different regimes to find the best and most stable parameters for the LPBF process – a single track with sufficient penetration (depth) into the underlying layer for good fusion, a good width for efficient processing (too narrow will require smaller hatch spacing requiring much longer build times) and stable, even and continuous track formation. When multiple tracks and layers are created to build 3D objects, the process remains stable resulting in a good part. The hatch spacing between adjacent tracks and the build height of successive layers must be optimized considering the size, depth and shape of the single track. Despite this optimization, various types of pores may be created in the LPBF process. Different pore formation mechanisms have been identified in LPBF, with the three most common forms related to process parameters being keyhole mode pores, metallurgical or gas pores and lack-of-fusion pores. Keyhole mode porosity occurs when the energy density is too high (e.g.

formation mechanisms have been identified in LPBF, with the three most common forms related to process parameters being keyhole mode pores, metallurgical or gas pores and lack-of-fusion pores. Keyhole mode porosity occurs when the energy density is too high (e.g. high power or slow speed), creating a vapour depression in the deep meltpool with high liquid flow velocity, which closes in on itself and traps vapours as the meltpool propagates forward. This results in large rounded but not perfectly spherical pores. Lack of fusion pores occur when the energy density is too low (low power or high speed), causing insufficient fusion of material and which results in irregular pores with varying sizes and with sharper edges and flattened shape, and sometimes containing entrapped unmelted particles. In the intermediate regime of energy density (conduction mode), gas porosity of very small sizes with spherical shape occurs and this has been attributed to gas entrapment of shielding gas, porosity of powder particles, or alloy vapours inside the molten pool. In addition, high scanning speeds coupled with insufficient laser power leads to balling effects in individual tracks due to meltpool viscosity and surface tension effects causing the track to break into beads, which can lead to uneven powder spreading and insufficient melting in the next layer.

with insufficient laser power leads to balling effects in individual tracks due to meltpool viscosity and surface tension effects causing the track to break into beads, which can lead to uneven powder spreading and insufficient melting in the next layer. On the other hand, low scanning speeds coupled with low power can lead to break-up of melt tracks due to the smaller meltpool near its threshold for melting thus not forming a continuous track. In addition to these effects, denudation zones where powder is removed from the front or sides of the meltpool due to meltpool dynamics or gas flow, can create uneven melting and irregular tracks, and particle spatter may also cause irregularity – for example the spatter falling on an unmelted area of the powder bed will shield the underlying powder from the laser. These and other causes of porosity and flaws in LPBF are described in more detail in. There have also been numerous efforts at modelling the meltpool dynamics which reveal information on pore formation, and on macroscale simulation for prediction of porosity formation . There have been high-resolution X-ray tomography imaging studies covering a range of process parameters showing lack of fusion and keyhole mode pores using synchrotron radiation.

There have been high-resolution X-ray tomography imaging studies covering a range of process parameters showing lack of fusion and keyhole mode pores using synchrotron radiation. The use of laboratory X-ray tomography for detailed analysis of AM materials was reviewed recently and also summarizes various efforts at imaging different porosity types. The analysis of porosity due to electron beam melting with different scan strategies was reported in and similarly for LPBF. Porosity formation has been studied in real time with great success using fast X-ray imaging with synchrotron sources. This includes studies of the meltpool dynamics showing keyhole pore formation and particle spatter, defect and meltpool dynamics showing pore formation mechanisms and effects of denudation zones, and threshold for keyhole vapour depression formation, the formation of keyhole mode pores at the end of scan tracks due to slowing of the laser and increased power density, amongst others. What is important to note is the recent discovery that the threshold for vapour depression formation is lower than expected and the fact that vapour depressions are therefore present across the entire range of typical LPBF process parameters, but that the formation of pores occurs only when the vapour depression becomes deep and unstable.

Many commercial systems already have an optimized process parameter set for typical AM materials such as Ti Al V, allowing near fully dense parts with minimal porosity. This results, in combination with suitable microstructure, in excellent mechanical properties as demonstrated for example in. However, an understanding of the porosity formation in 3D parts is useful to further improve the processes and find new ways of reducing the formation of these pores. Understanding the formation of porosity, pore shapes and distributions can assist in quality control efforts, to know what is wrong in the system, for appropriate corrective action (e.g. laser power drop causing lack of fusion pores). Since adjacent tracks and subsequent layers overlap, remelting occurs and this may contribute to minimizing porosity, or modifying the shape of the final resulting porosity distributions. An understanding of the resulting pore sizes and distributions is valuable for “forensic” analysis of porosity distributions found in built parts.

In this paper we report on a detailed X-ray tomography study of 5 mm cubes of Ti Al V produced under different process parameters of a commercial system. The aim of this study is to provide detail and insight into the typical porosity formation regimes and the resulting pore formation sizes, extents and shapes of pores over a large range of porosity values, for a typical LPBF system and for final built parts. Variation of laser power at different scan speeds show the regimes of lack-of-fusion, conduction mode and keyhole mode porosity with porosity levels all the way from below 0.0 % up to more than 1 %. The low-power threshold for lack of fusion porosity is very sharp and varies as expected for different scan speeds. Contrary to previous reports, the onset and increase in detectable keyhole mode porosity at increasing power is gradual, and not as sharp as the transition to lack-of-fusion. Comparing scan speeds, there is a stronger increase (higher slope) in keyhole mode porosity formation for slower scan speed.

and increase in detectable keyhole mode porosity at increasing power is gradual, and not as sharp as the transition to lack-of-fusion. Comparing scan speeds, there is a stronger increase (higher slope) in keyhole mode porosity formation for slower scan speed. This is in agreement with results of where it was shown that keyhole vapour depressions are suppressed at higher velocity. This shows that a larger window of “low porosity” is present in the case of faster scan speed, but the threshold for lack of fusion is higher, requiring a higher power laser (or smaller spot size). A secondary goal of this work was to produce a series of high quality 3D images of different pore types found in final produced parts, as the previously reported morphologies vary significantly, and a holistic collection of images from a range of process parameters for the same material on the same system is deemed valuable to an improved understanding and further development of the technology.

This is clearly seen in the first image in which shows slice images viewed from the top of the cube. As the power increases (in this series of images for 8 mm/s), there is an optimal value in terms of porosity reduction, followed by a gradual increase in randomly dispersed pores with roundish shapes. These roundish pores are attributed to keyhole mode porosity, when too high laser power causes a deep meltpool with strong meltpool dynamics causing entrapment of vapours in pores as the meltpool propagates forward and the vapour cavity collapses.

In the intermediate regime where porosity is minimized, small spherical pores are found only – these are presumably metallurgical pores which are due to trapped gas from between powders. In the series of images in it is clear that the keyhole mode porosity increases in extent with increasing power at 8 mm/s. The quantitative porosity values are plotted in, showing power variation for two scan speeds 1 mm/s and 8 mm/s. This shows, as expected, that the optimal power for minimal porosity is lower at lower scan speed. It can be understood that a slower moving beam requires less power to create the same size meltpool. What is interesting to note here is that for both scan speeds, there are clear thresholds below which lack of fusion become predominant, but no clear threshold for keyhole mode porosity formation.

This is consistent with recent high speed imaging work where it was vapour depressions are present in the meltpool for all process parameters above a threshold value, but that keyhole pores are only present when the vapour depression becomes unstable at higher power. What these results show additionally is that the resulting keyhole mode pores increase in extent and in size with increasing power – this is understandable as the higher power likely results in a larger vapour cavity. It is further interesting that the keyhole mode porosity formation increase with power (the slope) varies with scan speed. It seems higher power and speed allows a wider “safe” process parameter range with less extensive keyhole mode porosity – the higher speed suppresses instability in the vapour depression, also consistent with reports in. All data is provided and mesh representations of porosity of every cube is supplied. A comprehensive experimental campaign was conducted to visualize pore formation regimes in a typical commercial metal AM system using X-ray tomography. The results assist in understanding the typical porosity extent, pore shapes and distributions in a commercial LPBF system.

supplied. A comprehensive experimental campaign was conducted to visualize pore formation regimes in a typical commercial metal AM system using X-ray tomography. The results assist in understanding the typical porosity extent, pore shapes and distributions in a commercial LPBF system. The lowest porosity regime is shown to be in a narrow range, with the threshold for lack of fusion being very sharp and the formation of keyhole mode pores showing only a gradual increase with power. The keyhole pores increase in number and size with increasing power, but at higher scan speed the keyhole pore formation is suppressed and its onset is therefore only at higher power. The safe processing window is therefore larger at higher scan speed. Besides this, various other distributions of porosity have been visualized and described. Contour porosity was shown to occur due to larger spacing between hatch and contour tracks, with a similar distribution as that reported previously for a slowing beam at the end of scan tracks. Structured pores were demonstrated with a spacing at the track width and in lines at angles corresponding to the inter-layer angular rotation of the system. These results assist in providing more insight into pore formation mechanisms and may assist in correct diagnosis of porosity types in non-destructive test and quality control efforts.

Future work might include similar investigations of complex geometries, varied build angles, downskin surfaces and the potential for porosity variations with height corresponding to temperature build-up. Such work has the potential to yield practical solutions to mitigate porosity formation, by process parameter variations varied due to geometry of part.

Review on volumetric energy density: influence on morphology and mechanical properties of Ti Al V manufactured via laser powder bed fusion Various laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) process parameters must be considered as they can independently affect the properties of end-product. However, many studies simply examine one or two LPBF process parameters. Laser power, scan speed, scan spacing, and layer height are the four primary LPBF process parameters that contribute to volumetric energy density (VED) used in LPBF. VED is often used as an optimization metric for LPBF process parameters, because it takes all four major parameters into consideration. Thus, this paper focuses on the effect of VED on the morphology and properties of part, and also discusses on the interrelationship between all four parameters. Common range used for each parameter is 7 W for laser power, 7 mm/s for scan speed, 5 µm for scan spacing, and 2 µm for layer height. It can be seen as the VED increased, the microstructure of as-built titanium alloy Ti Al V components exhibited smaller α’ martensite size and larger columnar β grain. High VED can also reduce porosity and defect formation, which will help in increasing part density. The lowest surface roughness reported for LPBF Ti Al V is 4.9 µm.

components exhibited smaller α’ martensite size and larger columnar β grain. High VED can also reduce porosity and defect formation, which will help in increasing part density. The lowest surface roughness reported for LPBF Ti Al V is 4.9 µm. Meanwhile, the maximum microhardness obtained is 4 HV and the highest tensile strength achieved is 1 MPa. The VED used for studies that obtained these results are in the range of 5 J/mm . Thus, it can be the most suitable VED for LPBF printing of Ti Al V is around 5 J/mm . One of the nine pillars introduced in the fourth industrial revolution (IR 4.0) is three-dimensional (3D) printing or additive manufacturing (AM). The metal AM field offers the possibility of fabricating a wide range of materials with complex geometry features and applications, as stated by and. Moreover, metal AM technologies help in manufacturing parts that are difficult to machine as reported by. According to research conducted by ARC Advisory Group, aerospace, automotive, and defense industries are the largest markets for metal AM in 2 . The common practice for many automotive sector manufacturers with AM is prototyping purposes because conventional methods and AM have yet to coincide in a fully automated environment.

and defense industries are the largest markets for metal AM in 2 . The common practice for many automotive sector manufacturers with AM is prototyping purposes because conventional methods and AM have yet to coincide in a fully automated environment. Meanwhile, in the aerospace industry, such industry is more focused on printing designs with intricate details while using less material that aims for reducing fuel consumption. Furthermore, since parts can be fabricated on-site using AM technologies, the self-sufficiency nature of military operations can be supported as mentioned by. Metal 3D printing is also prevalent in the medical industry as and the technology aids in individualization (e.g., customized implants) and surgical cost reduction. According to ISO/ASTM 5 :2 , technologies in AM can be categorized into seven categories, including four metal AM technologies which are powder bed fusion (PBF), direct energy deposition (DED), material jetting, and binder jetting. and among the four technologies, only are already at high technology readiness level (TRL). Laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) is one of the commercially available PBF process, alongside electron powder bed fusion (EPBF). LPBF is also known as selective laser melting (SLM), direct metal laser sintering (DMLS), and LaserCUSING, which are trademarked terms by SLM Solutions Group AG, EOS GmbH, and Concept Laser, respectively.

available PBF process, alongside electron powder bed fusion (EPBF). LPBF is also known as selective laser melting (SLM), direct metal laser sintering (DMLS), and LaserCUSING, which are trademarked terms by SLM Solutions Group AG, EOS GmbH, and Concept Laser, respectively. In this paper, only the term LPBF is used to avoid confusion. Among the various methods in the metal AM field, LPBF is a promising technique due to its numerous advantages. First, reported LPBF can produce top quality parts from metal powders as it allows homogeneous build-up to almost full density. Besides, the LPBF system acknowledges a wide range of materials for fabrication such as titanium (Ti), stainless steel, aluminium (Al), cobalt-chromium (CoCr), and even gold (Au), as stated by. The performance of these LPBF-fabricated parts is comparable to those produced using conventional methods, which will be discussed in Sect. 5. However, due to large thermal gradient present in LPBF, it can introduce huge residual stresses and further explanation can be found in studies done by,, and. The first step of LPBF process begins with the development of a 3D model using a computer-aided design (CAD) software or produced using a 3D scanner. A support structure for the model will be generated and this model will then be sliced into a stack of two-dimensional (2D) layers. The stack of 2D layers, which consists of both internal and external geometry features, will then be loaded into the LPBF machine to begin the printing process. A thin layer of metal powder will be uniformly deposited on the substrate plate using a roller before a focused laser beam melts the powder bed. After the plate is lowered down by one-layer thickness, a new layer will be applied and melted to form a successive layer that is metallurgically bonded with the previous layer. This process will be repeated until a 3D object or component is fully built. To avoid oxidation and contamination, this printing process is done with high purity shielding gas.

Due to rapid repeated heating and cooling process, high internal residual stresses are likely and can be countered by pre-heating the build plate or heat treating the end-product. One of commonly fabricated materials using LPBF is Ti Al V alloy, or also known as Ti alloy which consists of Ti, Al, and vanadium (V). This alloy is an American Society for (ASTM) Grade 5 and Grade 2 Ti. Ti Grade 2 is the higher purity version of Ti Grade 5, as it contains lower levels of oxygen (O), nitrogen (Ni), carbon (C), and iron (Fe) compared to Ti grade 5. Ti Grade 2 is also known as Ti Al V Extra Low Interstitials (ELI). It is noteworthy that in this paper, Ti Al V alloy refers to both Ti grade. stated Ti Al V is an alpha (α)-beta (β) Ti alloy that is widely used in biomedical applications, such as prosthesis and joint replacement due to this biomaterial’s low density, high strength-to-weight ratio, high corrosion resistance, excellent wear properties, and most importantly, high biocompatibility.

The LPBF-fabricated parts’ density depends on the chemical composition and the resulting porosity from the process. The density can be measured using the Archimedes, pycnometer, or optical methods. In theory, LPBF can fabricate a fully dense part as mentioned by. However, due to insubstantial process parameters, pores will always be present. The only difference is whether the porosity level is manageable because an uncontrolled porosity level can initiate microcracks and adiabatic shear bands, which can lead to poor performance of fabricated parts as stated by. Industrial application, especially for a loadbearing structural part, requires a high part density (which will contribute to high mechanical performance) induced by the low porosity level.

Meanwhile, Hudák et al.,, and applications such as Ti Al V implants need a controlled porous structure to reduce the “stress-shielding” effect. Stress shielding happens when bone density is decreased as a result of an implant removing stress from the bone. Pores are formed in LPBF-fabricated parts due to the liquid–solid phase transformation during the solidification process. Two types of pores can be present in LPBF parts, i.e., gas pores and lack-of-fusion pores, . Gas pores are characterized by their spherical or elliptical shapes, while lack-of-fusion pores are often larger and have irregular shapes with some sharp tips. On one hand, gas pores are present when gas bubbles could not escape the molten pool in time due to the rapid solidification induced by the high VED, causing them to be trapped in the solidified parts as mentioned by. On the other hand, lack-of-fusion pores form due to insufficient VED (e.g., too low laser power or too high scan speed) to melt a disproportionate amount of powders. Consequently, inadequate melting and incomplete interlayer bonding may occur.
