Lack-of-fusion pores are more dangerous than gas pores, because they can greatly influence the mechanical properties of the fabricated parts since these pores are usually distributed at the boundary zone of two adjacent layers, which are typically aligned in the building direction as reported by. mentioned that when the parts are under a uniaxial tensile load parallel to the building direction, the sharp tips of the lack-of-fusion pores will accumulate stresses and act as a crack initiator, leading to premature failure. These pores can be reduced by increasing the VED used to melt the powder layers. Among all the AM technologies, LPBF is one of the promising methods and it has been extensively studied by researchers over the past few years. Since past research conducted has managed to fabricate high-density end-products, the focus of LPBF studies has changed the direction toward the optimization of select process parameters. A common optimization metrics used is the VED of LPBF. This is because it consists of four dominant process parameters which are laser power, scan speed, scan spacing, and layer height. VED is utilized to successively melt layers of metal powder, which will then lead to the fabrication of parts with diverse microstructure and mechanical properties . 4 and 5. concludes VED ranges that are the most optimum to obtain Ti Al V parts with excellent properties. Nevertheless, the VED is not the sole factor that will determine the properties of the LPBF-fabricated parts.

Nevertheless, the VED is not the sole factor that will determine the properties of the LPBF-fabricated parts. This is because the formula used to calculate VED only include four process parameters while dismissing critical factors such as laser spot diameter and scan strategy. Besides, two different parameter sets can contribute to a similar amount of VED, while producing parts that have different mechanical performance. Therefore, some future research directions that can be considered are: 1. The interdependencies of all four parameters (i.e., laser power, scan speed, scan spacing, and layer height). This can be done to investigate the reliability of VED as an optimization metric for LPBF; because different parameter combinations can induce different thermal behaviors which will then vary the microstructure and performance of the end-products. 2. The standardization of powder properties. This must be conducted before a patterned LPBF processing window for Ti Al V parts can be produced. Currently, the powder used in studies depends on the machine model used since most LPBF manufacturers provide their own powders. This leads to inconsistency in the performance of the fabricated part due to different powder properties.

As such, the optimized process parameters obtained might not be applicable for different LPBF machine models. 3. The evolution of microstructure before and after postprocessing. Although the presence of α’ martensite in the as-built part aids in the corrosion resistance of Ti Al V, columnar β grain structures are required to improve the part’s tensile strength. Thus, post-processing is usually done to transform the martensitic phase in order to counter defects, even though theoretically, LPBF can alter the microstructure during printing process to obtain the desired part properties.

Title: Increasing productivity for laser powder bed fusion of Ti–6Al–4V parts through increased layer thickness Additive manufacturing (AM) has enabled the development of numerous innovative products that are now commercially available. However, its adoption for producing components traditionally manufactured using conventional methods remains limited. A significant barrier to broader industrial implementation of AM techniques, such as laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF), is their comparatively lower productivity rates, directly impacting manufacturing costs and, consequently, the adoption rate of L-PBF technology. This study examines whether modifications to L-PBF process parameters can mitigate cost-related constraints associated with production rates whilst maintaining the material properties of Ti–6Al–4V produced at standard layer thicknesses. Whilst existing research on Ti–6Al–4V processing has primarily focussed on microstructural and mechanical properties, there has been limited investigation into productivity enhancements through increased layer thicknesses beyond the conventional range of 2 µm. To address this gap, a series of experiments were conducted to evaluate the feasibility of increasing the layer thickness to 1 µm by modifying key process parameters, including laser power, scan speed, and hatch distance. The L-PBF parameters were varied through a response surface methodology (RSM) design of experiments (DOE) to obtain, via statistical analysis, the optimum parameters for Ti–6Al–4V at 1 μm layer thicknesses.

process parameters, including laser power, scan speed, and hatch distance. The L-PBF parameters were varied through a response surface methodology (RSM) design of experiments (DOE) to obtain, via statistical analysis, the optimum parameters for Ti–6Al–4V at 1 μm layer thicknesses. The findings demonstrate that L-PBF systems can sustain a stable processing environment whilst operating at higher throughput levels. This was established through experimental results that show the DOE process identifying L-PBF parameters that can produce acceptable bulk density at an increased layer thickness of 1 μm. Furthermore, mechanical testing revealed that specimens printed at 1 µm exhibited comparable mechanical properties to those fabricated at the standard 6 µm thickness, achieving an average ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of 1 MPa and 1 MPa, and a median elongation of 9.2 % and 9.7 %, respectively. Post-processing techniques, including vacuum heat treatment and hot isostatic pressing (HIP), further enhanced mechanical properties, ensuring that all 1 µm specimens met or exceeded ASTM F - 1 (Designation: F Standard specification for additive manufacturing titanium–6 aluminum–4 vanadium ELI (extra low interstitial) with powder bed fusion. 2 . https://doi.org/1 .1 /F ) requirements.

mechanical properties, ensuring that all 1 µm specimens met or exceeded ASTM F - 1 (Designation: F Standard specification for additive manufacturing titanium–6 aluminum–4 vanadium ELI (extra low interstitial) with powder bed fusion. 2 . https://doi.org/1 .1 /F ) requirements. This study highlights the potential for increasing L-PBF productivity without compromising material performance, offering a pathway towards more cost-effective AM production for aerospace and biomedical applications 1 Introduction The laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF) additive manufacturing (AM) process has been adopted by various regulated industries. The design freedom and opportunities it offers, enable designers and engineers to create complex, high-value products. However, scaling L-PBF for mass production of less complex components has proven challenging due to the high costs associated with adopting the technology relative to machine output. Reducing the build time for each part can significantly lower production costs, making L-PBF more viable for broader industrial applications. Ti–6Al–4V is amongst the most widely used L-PBF materials, particularly in high-value, high-volume industries such as aerospace and medical devices. Consequently, extensive research has been conducted on L-PBF Ti–6Al–4V, focussing on optimising process parameters to enhance density, mechanical performance, and microstructural properties. For example, used various experimental models and neural networking to optimise parameter sets for specific mechanical properties including surface roughness and hardness.

The trend of densities between sample sets is maintained across the two testing methods indicating repeatability with a potential lack of accuracy. The sources of this inaccuracy can be explained by aspects of the samples being tested that affect the Archimedes method and assumptions in the optical microscopy testing method. First, varying surface roughness across a sample set can affect the repeatability of Archimedes testing and subsequently report lower-than-expected density values. In the case of the 1 µm samples, the surface is rougher than that of the 6 µm, with Ra values of 7.3 µm and 7.1 µm, respectively. The samples used in the study were not polished at the DOE screening phase as it was not practical to polish numerous small samples to a consistent surface finish. To account for this, the Archimedes testing procedure was repeated multiple times ensuring little to no air bubbles were caught on the surface of the samples. In addition, the optical microscopy testing garnered density values where surface roughness was not a factor. By employing a response surface methodology (RSM) DOE, the study was able to identify the optimal set of parameters for achieving high productivity whilst maintaining material properties. The DOE process involved varying key parameters such as laser power, scan speed, and hatch distance within a defined volumetric energy density (VED) range. This approach enabled the identification of interparameter relationships and the establishment of a robust process window for L-PBF of Ti–6Al–4V at increased layer thicknesses.

Title: Effects of porosity and microstructure on the fatigue fracture properties of ti al v alloy produced through selective laser melting Selective laser melting (SLM) was employed to fabricate Ti Al V samples, focusing on laser energy density ranges suitable for industrial applications. The process involved nine levels of laser energy density (6 .6 .0 J/mm ) to achieve full density in the SLM-fabricated products. Experimental results the Archimedes relative density of the specimens increased to 9 .8 %. At this density level, characterized by minimal porosity, microstructural analysis revealed the presence of pores both on the surface and within the internal regions. The various laser energy density levels, including lower, higher, and optimal values, promoted the αˈ-phase microstructure, thereby affecting the microhardness. X-ray diffractometer (XRD) analysis confirmed the presence of acicular αˈ-phases in the SLM Ti Al V components. Fatigue tests, including high-cycle fatigue (HCF) and low-cycle fatigue (LCF), were conducted on the samples exhibiting minimal porosity and refined microstructure, yielding fatigue limits of 1 MPa and lifetimes up to 1 cycles. The results fatigue cracks in the Ti Al V alloy predominantly originated from surface-connected pores. The relationships between microstructure, porosity, and fractography revealed that the rapid cooling in the SLM process limited crystal growth, leading to smaller crystal sizes and influencing fatigue performance.

cracks in the Ti Al V alloy predominantly originated from surface-connected pores. The relationships between microstructure, porosity, and fractography revealed that the rapid cooling in the SLM process limited crystal growth, leading to smaller crystal sizes and influencing fatigue performance. The findings highlight the practical significance of optimizing laser energy density in improving the mechanical and fatigue properties of SLM-fabricated Ti Al V components by reducing porosity and promoting the formation of strong metallurgical bonds in the microstructure. Additive manufacturing (AM) refers to the process of producing three-dimensional objects through the layer-bylayer addition of material, guided by digital 3D data. AM technologies share common components, including 3D modeling software, equipment, and powdered materials. One such method, selective laser melting (SLM), is based on the principle of melting metal powders with a laser to shape them, and it is one of the most widely used AM techniques in this field. SLM allows for the direct production of objects with complex geometries, eliminating the need for additional post-production processes with significant material removal and modifications. However, it should be noted that AM techniques often involve associated post-processing requirements, such as support removal, grinding, polishing, or chemical milling, which can require substantial effort.

However, it should be noted that AM techniques often involve associated post-processing requirements, such as support removal, grinding, polishing, or chemical milling, which can require substantial effort. Laser additive manufacturing processes, particularly for Ti-6Al-4 V used in aerospace applications, offer significant potential for improving mechanical performance and reducing fabrication costs, while also contributing to environmental sustainability through decreased fuel consumption and optimized material efficiency. Ti Al V is a two-phase titanium alloy known for its excellent mechanical properties, corrosion resistance, and biocompatibility. This alloy is widely used in various application areas, including aerospace, gas turbines, marine vessels, and the medical industry. Conventional Ti Al V production processes typically involve machining cast or forged billets. However, for parts with complex geometries, these methods often result in significant material waste, with more than 5 % of the billet material potentially discarded, making them inefficient in terms of cost and production time. In this context, AM methods, particularly SLM, are emerging as a more effective alternative for the production of Ti Al V alloy. Ti Al V is an α+β alloy containing 6 wt% Al as an α-stabilizer and 4 wt% V as a β-stabilizer.

AM methods, particularly SLM, are emerging as a more effective alternative for the production of Ti Al V alloy. Ti Al V is an α+β alloy containing 6 wt% Al as an α-stabilizer and 4 wt% V as a β-stabilizer. Under typical conditions, the microstructure consists of approximately 9 % α phase (hexagonal close-packed) at room temperature after slow cooling from the β phase region, while around 1 % remains as metastable β phase (body-centered cubic). SLM is characterized by high heating and cooling rates; for instance, in a typical SLM process, the heating rate reaches approximately 1 –1 °K/s, while the cooling rate ranges from 1 to 1 °K/s. Cooling rates significantly influence the internal structure of the material, often resulting in the formation of a high proportion (9 %) of α' (martensitic-α) phase within the β matrix. Under slower cooling conditions, the α' phase can decompose into α and β phases, depending on the cooling rate. Despite the many advantages of SLM, there are notable metallurgical differences in parts produced by this method. These differences become evident when compared to those manufactured using conventional processes. These differences include mechanical anisotropy, residual stresses, and defects inherent to AM process.

The effects of the SLM process on the final product are determined by various process parameters, including laser power, scanning strategy, layer thickness, scanning speed, and scanning interval. These parameters play a crucial role in influencing the material’s microstructural development and bulk density Additionally, they have a significant impact on the strength and hardness of the final product. The effects of these parameters on product quality have been extensively studied in the literature. For example, laser power and scanning strategy have been shown to significantly influence the material’s properties and have a pronounced impact on the resulting microstructure. Other studies highlight the role of laser energy density in determining porosity levels. Additionally, the interplay between scanning speed and layer thickness has also been investigated for its effect on mechanical properties.

Laser energy density plays a critical role in the selective laser melting (SLM) process, as it directly influences the quality and performance of the final parts. Insufficient energy density caused by low laser power, high scanning speed, or large hatch spacing can result in incomplete melting of the substrate and layers, leading to the “balling” phenomenon and increased porosity in the samples. Conversely, overly small hatch spacing may cause excessive energy concentration, leading to overheating and pore formation, while excessively high energy density can induce keyhole pores and irregular laser tracks. Therefore, maintaining laser energy density within an optimal range is essential for minimizing defects such as porosity and ensuring high-density, high-performance parts. This highlights the importance of energy density optimization in achieving reliable and highquality SLM-fabricated components. Selective laser melting (SLM) produces titanium alloy parts with superior tensile strength, up to 2 % higher than conventionally manufactured counterparts. However, the presence of higher residual stresses, surface roughness, and porosity in SLM-fabricated parts can significantly reduce their fatigue life compared to traditionally produced parts.

However, the presence of higher residual stresses, surface roughness, and porosity in SLM-fabricated parts can significantly reduce their fatigue life compared to traditionally produced parts. During the SLM process, the rapid heating and cooling cycles lead to grain growth that is dependent on direction, as well as the development of internal porosity and anisotropic microstructures. As each layer cools from its molten state, it shrinks and creates stress on the previously fused layer. Although minimizing internal porosity is important, the roughness of the surface topography can significantly impact fatigue strength by serving as nucleation sites for fatigue cracks. For SLM-produced components subjected to cyclic loading, it is essential to investigate not only the parameters of processing and post-processing treatments but also their influence on porosity, microstructure, and the resulting fatigue characteristics of Ti Al V-based components. Additionally, while earlier research on the fatigue behavior of porous biomaterials has primarily concentrated on compressive fatigue resistance, it is equally important to assess fatigue under tensile loads. In many load-bearing scenarios, especially in the context of medical implants, compression is often the primary loading mechanism, but bio-implants can also experience tensile loads due to the unpredictable nature of forces exerted by the human body.

In many load-bearing scenarios, especially in the context of medical implants, compression is often the primary loading mechanism, but bio-implants can also experience tensile loads due to the unpredictable nature of forces exerted by the human body. While many studies have primarily used quasistatic compression or tension–compression tests to assess structural integrity , real-world applications subject implanted materials to significant cyclic loading throughout their lifespan, often involving repeated tensile stresses. Thus, evaluating both low and high-cycle fatigue behavior, especially under tension-tension loading conditions, is crucial when designing new metallic biomaterials. A common drawback of SLM-produced materials is their ower fatigue strength compared to conventionally machined parts. This reduced fatigue performance is mainly due to the presence of significant surface roughness and residual micro-porosity within the material. Despite extensive studies on the fatigue performance of Ti Al V alloys produced via the SLM method, certain significant gaps remain in the literature. While the critical influence of laser energy density on material formation, porosity, and grain growth has been widely addressed, its effect on tension-tension fatigue performance has been explored only to a limited extent. Ti Al V alloy holds significant potential as a biomaterial due to its inherent properties and the SLM method’s capability to produce components with complex geometries and high precision.

In the SLM process, laser energy density is calculated as a combination of four key parameters: laser power, scanning speed, layer thickness, and hatch spacing. The careful selection of these parameters directly impacts the material’s microstructure, porosity, and mechanical properties. However, studies investigating these parameters within the context of industrially adopted energy density ranges and their correlation with fatigue performance remain insufficient. Additionally, the effects of domestically developed additive manufacturing (AM) systems, such as the ENA 2 machine, on material properties and performance have been rarely examined in the literature. This study provides a detailed investigation of the effects of laser energy density on microstructure, porosity, relative density, and microhardness, as well as an evaluation of tension-tension fatigue performance based on these parameters.

Conducted using the domestically developed ENA 2 system, this study contributes to the optimization of SLM processes and offers a new perspective for both industrial and biomedical applications. 3 Results and discussion In the study, relative density, surface roughness, microstructure, and phase properties were prioritized to improve the performance of the samples. The microstructural images reveal a strong metallurgical bond under optimal parameters, with no evidence of features corresponding to the scanning interval, likely due to the 9 ° scanning angle. Columnar prior β grains surrounding the α' martensite are distinctly visible in the cross sections ( a, c, and e), illustrating a typical characteristic of the SLM process. Within these columnar grains, a significant quantity of fine needle-like α' laths was observed, with size variations depending on energy density. Most α′ laths nucleate at the boundaries of the previous β grains and grow within the primary β grains; however, a few α′ martensites extend into adjacent β grains, which may occur due to twin grain boundaries or very similar orientations between the neighboring β grains. It was the needle-like α' laths becomes thinner as the energy density (Ev) increases ( b, d, and f). During SLM, a higher Ev generates a greater temperature gradient and cooling rate.

During SLM, a higher Ev generates a greater temperature gradient and cooling rate. The increased cooling rate associated with a higher energy density (Ev) not only promotes a faster nucleation rate of α' martensite but also limits the time available for coarsening, resulting in thinner α' laths. This rapid cooling is critical for the presence of the martensitic phase (α') in the as-built condition of LPBF-produced Ti Al V samples, as opposed to wrought materials where slower cooling rates preclude the formation of α' without very rapid quenching. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis has been extensively employed to investigate the changes in the crystal structure of Ti Al V alloys produced by powder metallurgy and selective laser melting (SLM) methods. The Ti Al V alloy typically consists of a mixture of α (alpha phase with a hexagonal close-packed structure) and β (body-centered cubic structure) phases. The α' phase forms during martensitic transformation and can exhibit higher dislocation densities than the α phase, resulting in broader peaks in XRD analysis. Distinguishing between the α and α' phases using XRD can be challenging due to the small differences in lattice parameters.

Distinguishing between the α and α' phases using XRD can be challenging due to the small differences in lattice parameters. Additionally, the α' phase often displays a smaller c/a ratio when saturated with β-stabilizing elements, such as aluminum and vanadium. The XRD spectra for both powder and SLM samples are . The analysis results indicate that both the powder and SLM samples exhibit a single hcp phase, confirming the presence of the α' martensite phase. The generally single-phase characteristic of gas-atomized powders can be attributed to the high cooling rates experienced during production. The observed shift of the diffraction peaks to lower angles during the SLM process suggests a distortion in the crystal lattice. This distortion can be attributed to the rapid solidification of the β phase above the beta-transus temperature, coupled with insufficient diffusion of solid-solution atoms (such as vanadium) from the unit cells. Consequently, this leads to increased lattice distortions and higher dislocation densities. Non-uniform strain affects both the broadening and intensity of the XRD peaks, while uniform strain influences their position. Equations (2) and (3) from the uniform deformation model are employed to calculate the shifts in the XRD peaks, and the dislocation density is also determined.

(ii) Fusion failures: These typically occur due to insufficient laser energy density during production, leading to weak metallurgical bonds resulting from the partial melting of the final solidified layer. As a consequence of these weak bonds, irregular pores can form at the layer interfaces. For example, fluctuations in laser beam power can lead to melting issues, resulting in unmelted regions that exhibit varying morphologies on the fracture surfaces due to their occurrence between two layers. These defects are more critical than porosity. (iii) Surface defects: These are the most critical for fatigue performance, as they create favorable conditions for crack initiation. Surface defects can arise from surface pores, unmelted areas, or high surface roughness in certain sections. Additionally, residual stresses and microstructural inhomogeneities are significant factors influencing the initiation life of fatigue cracks. Some studies have identified internal defects as the primary cause of fractures in high-cycle fatigue, while others suggest that this is only applicable to very short-life specimens. In contrast, longer-life specimens typically exhibit crack initiation at the surface.

This study demonstrates that the fatigue behavior of SLMed Ti Al V parts is strongly affected by surface and subsurface defects, with machining and appropriate surface treatments proving to be critical. In this study, the influence of laser energy density on the microhardness, microstructure, and phase transformations of Ti Al V alloy fabricated via selective laser melting (SLM) was examined. Additionally, the high-cycle fatigue behavior and fracture characteristics of the SLMed Ti Al V samples were analyzed. The key findings from this investigation are as follows: 1) The effects of variations in laser energy density on the formation of microstructural defects and their impact on fatigue behavior were investigated. The selected energy levels encompassed ranges suitable for industrial applications while also exploring levels that have not been extensively studied before. 2) The relative density increased to values ranging from 9 .7 to 9 .8 % as Ev decreased between 7 and 9 J/mm but dropped to 9 % when Ev exceeded 1 J/ mm .

not been extensively studied before. 2) The relative density increased to values ranging from 9 .7 to 9 .8 % as Ev decreased between 7 and 9 J/mm but dropped to 9 % when Ev exceeded 1 J/ mm . Variations in laser energy led to the formation of different pore types, including sharp crack-like pores, micro-porosities, and lack-of-fusion areas. At an energy density of 7 J/mm , which yielded the highest density, only a small number of micro-porosity-type pores were observed. 3) The SLM process resulted in the formation of various crystallographic phases, including α' (hcp martensite) and β (bcc). The optical microstructure of the SLMed Ti Al V revealed the presence of acicular martensite within columnar prior β grains. The needle-like α' laths within the columnar grains were found to refine with increasing Ev. 4) XRD peak analysis confirmed a fully martensitic α' structure with a high dislocation density in the as-built samples. Additionally, quantitative calculations revealed that the crystallite size of the α' martensite in the SLMformed lattice structure was approximately 7 nm. 5) Microhardness was observed to correlate with the refinement of α' martensitic lamellae. As a finer microstructure formed at higher energy densities, the overall hardness of the material increased.

α' martensite in the SLMformed lattice structure was approximately 7 nm. 5) Microhardness was observed to correlate with the refinement of α' martensitic lamellae. As a finer microstructure formed at higher energy densities, the overall hardness of the material increased. 6) Under the production conditions used in this study, the tensile fatigue strength of SLM Ti Al V components was determined to be 1 MPa at 1 cycles. Near-surface defects such as lack-of-fusion zones and concentrated pores served as crack initiation sites in the SLMed components. These defects, even in samples with a relative density of 9 .7 %, negatively impacted fatigue life by acting as stress concentrators. 7) The findings of this study offer practical guidance for optimizing laser energy density to minimize defects and enhance the fatigue performance of SLM-fabricated Ti Al V components, which is critical for applications in aerospace and biomedical industries. 8) This study, which examines the fatigue behavior as a result of the effects of SLM parameters on microstructure development, provides useful insights for evaluating the performance of SLM-produced components in loadbearing applications

Impact of surface and pore characteristics on fatigue life of laser powder bed fusion Ti–6Al–4V alloy described by neural network models In this study, the effects of surface roughness and pore characteristics on fatigue lives of laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) Ti–6Al–4V parts were investigated. The 1 fatigue bars were printed using the same laser power but with varied scanning speeds. These actions led to variations in the geometries of microscale pores, and such variations were characterized using micro-computed tomography. To generate differences in surface roughness in fatigue bars, half of the samples were grit-blasted and the other half were machined. Fatigue behaviors were analyzed with respect to surface roughness and statistics of the pores. For the grit-blasted samples, the contour laser scan in the LPBF strategy led to a pore-depletion zone isolating surface and internal pores with different features. For the machined samples, where surface pores resemble internal pores, the fatigue life was highly correlated with the average pore size and projected pore area in the plane perpendicular to the stress direction. Finally, a machine learning model using a drop-out neural network (DONN) was employed to establish a link between surface and pore features to the fatigue data (logN), and good prediction accuracy was demonstrated.

Besides predicting fatigue lives, the DONN can also estimate the prediction uncertainty Introduction: Laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) is an additive manufacturing (AM) technique that uses high-power laser heating to melt and fuse fine metallic particles layer-by-layer in a powder bed . It offers an excellent opportunity for providing more versatility and efficiency in diverse environments as sustainment facilities and hospitals for asset replacement . LPBF can result in drastic improvements in shortening component development/refurbishment time, reducing the length and complexity of the supply chain, reducing inventory levels, replacing a medical device or returning an asset to operational service in a much shorter time, and ultimately resulting in an overall reduction of the cost of asset replacement and sustainment, and improved service. However, uncertainty and perceived risk associated with the mechanical properties of the products from this new technology have slowed its adoption to take advantage of its merits. Uncertainties are typically associated with concerns surrounding the processing and resultant material of a component.

and perceived risk associated with the mechanical properties of the products from this new technology have slowed its adoption to take advantage of its merits. Uncertainties are typically associated with concerns surrounding the processing and resultant material of a component. Factors such as laser power, scanning speed, powder contamination, process interruptions, unremoved powder, laser instability and surface finishing can all impact the microstructure of the printed material, leading to engineering uncertainty in the mechanical performance of the printed part . As a result, industry takes a relatively conservative stance in the acceptance of LPBF materials and printed components, and thus slow the widespread adoption of this technology for innovations. A number of studies have been conducted to overcome the engineering uncertainty or understand the underlying failure mechanism for LPBF parts. For instance, 1 have AM Ti–6Al–4V (Ti ) parts produced by high power laser can improve the fatigue lives after solution treatment and aging. 1 and 8 focused on the aspects related to the influence of surface roughness on the fatigue life by comparing LPBF and electron beam melting (EBM) technologies. 1 have the transformation of martensitic microstructure and variations of mechanical properties of Ti depend on post heat treatment.

aspects related to the influence of surface roughness on the fatigue life by comparing LPBF and electron beam melting (EBM) technologies. 1 have the transformation of martensitic microstructure and variations of mechanical properties of Ti depend on post heat treatment. The fundamental findings from these studies still remain to be translated to optimization strategies for the LPBF processes to achieve better material properties. It would be ideal to have the capability to control the LPBF-printed material properties by controlling machine process parameters in the printing process. However, with the diversity of LPBF machines and raw material powders available and the uncertainties related to the operating conditions (e.g., laser stability and powder contamination), it is virtually impossible to establish a universal correlation between process parameters and material properties. In addition, it is difficult to benchmark a process with respect to certain properties, such as fatigue tests, since the relevant tests require a large number of samples and are time-consuming. On the other hand, non-intrusive characterization of porous structures can be done in a much faster manner and consumes much less resources. If one can establish a robust correlation between the porosity of the LPBF-printed materials and their mechanical properties, it is then possible to use non-intrusive characterizations, such as a computer tomography (CT) scan, to evaluate if the part is acceptable.

If one can establish a robust correlation between the porosity of the LPBF-printed materials and their mechanical properties, it is then possible to use non-intrusive characterizations, such as a computer tomography (CT) scan, to evaluate if the part is acceptable. Such correlations have been studied in the literature using physics-based modeling techniques . However, such models also demand high computational resources and their accuracy depends on input parameters (e.g., melt pool size, crystalline orientation), which usually require extensive experiments to determine. Moreover, the integration of pores into such simulations is not trivial as this is limited by the finite domain size the model can handle. It is thus desirable to have simple surrogate models to avoid the above-mentioned challenges, and data-driven models can be a potential solution. To address some of the above-mentioned challenges, in this study, we investigated the effects of surface roughness and pore characteristics of LPBF-printed Ti parts on their fatigue lives and established data-driven surrogate models for their relationships as briefly outlined in. 1 fatigue bars were printed using a metal AM system (3D Systems ProX 3 ) with varying the laser scanning speed, which altered the local melting and fusion process of the powder and in turn led to variable porous structures.

1 fatigue bars were printed using a metal AM system (3D Systems ProX 3 ) with varying the laser scanning speed, which altered the local melting and fusion process of the powder and in turn led to variable porous structures. The pores were then characterized using micro-CT. To investigate the surface roughness effect, half of the samples were grit-blasted, and the other half were machine-finished, which are characterized using optical surface profilometry. The statistics of the micro-pore density, location, size and shape, as well as surface roughness were systematically analyzed. Selected samples were then mechanically tested for their fatigue properties. The correlations between surface and pore features and fatigue properties were analyzed. Finally, a machine learning model using a drop-out neural network (DONN) was trained to link the porosity and surface roughness to the fatigue data. Besides predicting fatigue life, the DONN also has the unique capability of estimating the prediction uncertainty. The evaluation of fatigue life given pore and surface input data only take a few seconds using this DONN model. Practically, such efficient surrogate models may serve to reduce the amount of physical testing needed for LPBF-generated components by informing the user if the property of a printed component is within acceptance limits. In this work, we investigated the effects of surface roughness and pore characteristics on the stress-controlled fatigue lives of direct LPBF-printed Ti fatigue bars, and developed machine learning models to describe their correlations. The unique feature leveraged in this study, the depletion zone achieved through the contour laser scan, played an essential role in separating the effect of pores and surface roughness. The contour laser scans in the LPBF process make AB samples have similar surface roughness, but diverse internal pore features were achieved by varying the laser scanning speed during the hatch scans. According to the linear-log model, narrow distribution (R =0.9 ) for all AB samples was . Therefore, this result suggests that the fatigue life of the AB samples is dominated by the microscale surface roughness (Ra~7.7 µm) regardless of the internal pore features. The M samples, which have internal pores exposed to the surface after machining, exhibit more scattered S–N plots among samples printed with different laser speeds. This result suggests that the fatigue life of the M samples is largely impacted by the pore features, which are influenced by the laser speed during the LPBF process.

This result suggests that the fatigue life of the M samples is largely impacted by the pore features, which are influenced by the laser speed during the LPBF process. A machine learning model using DONN was established to predict the quantitative relationship between the surface roughness, pore features and the fatigue data. The DONN-predicted average fatigue life agreed well with the experimentally measured values, with Pearson Correlation Coefficients of 0.9 and 0.9 , respectively, for the M and AB samples. DONN also has the unique capability of estimating the prediction uncertainty. The estimated prediction uncertainties were below 0.3 for the M samples and below 0.1 for the AB samples. Therefore, we expect that our data-driven surrogate model will contribute to advancing the LPBF process for industrial adoption by providing a fast evaluation of the acceptance of a printed part without the need for timeconsuming destructive tests.

Effects of Process Defects on the Flexural Fatigue Life of Ti-6Al-4V Fabricated by Laser Powder Bed Fusion Abstract: The fatigue performance of laser powder bed fusion-fabricated Ti-6Al-4V alloy was investigated using four-point bending testing. Specifically, the effects of keyhole and lack-of-fusion porosities along with various surface roughness parameters, were evaluated in the context of pore circularity and size using 2D optical metallography. Surface roughness of Sa = 1 to 7 microns was examined by SEM, and the corresponding fatigue performance was found to vary by 1 cycles to failure. The S–N curves for the various defects were also correlated with process window examination in laser beam power–velocity (P–V) space. Basquin’s stress-life relation was well fitted to the experimental S–N curves for various process parameters except keyhole porosity, indicating reduced importance for LPBF-fabricated Ti-6Al-4V alloy components. Ti-6Al-4V alloy has been one of the most studied alloys fabricated by AM, especially LPBF. Conclusions This study has comprehensively evaluated the fatigue performance of LPBF-as-built Ti-6Al-4V alloy components through four-point bending fatigue testing. The findings underscore the significant impact of surface finish and internal porosity (especially LoF) characteristics on the fatigue life of these components.

This study has comprehensively evaluated the fatigue performance of LPBF-as-built Ti-6Al-4V alloy components through four-point bending fatigue testing. The findings underscore the significant impact of surface finish and internal porosity (especially LoF) characteristics on the fatigue life of these components. Notably, the optimization of surface roughness to a Sa value of 7 microns from an initial 1 microns emerged as a pivotal factor in enhancing fatigue life performance, achieving ~1 cycles to failure at a stress amplitude of ~3 MPa. This improvement highlights the critical role of surface conditions in fatigue resistance. A comparison of prominent surface roughness parameters also indicated that the average surface roughness, Sa, provides an adequate representation of surface roughness. Moreover, the present research delineated the complex interplay between surface roughness, pore circularity, and size, further elucidating their collective influence on the detrimental effects of keyhole and lack-of-fusion porosities on fatigue performance. Such intricacies point to the necessity of a nuanced understanding of material properties and defect morphology in predicting and improving the fatigue behavior of additive manufactured components. Building on the foundations laid by previous studies, the present work reinforces the importance of careful manipulation of power–velocity (P–V) parameters within the designated process window of P–V space. By doing so, it is possible to significantly optimize the fatigue performance of as-built Ti-6Al-4V alloy components.

previous studies, the present work reinforces the importance of careful manipulation of power–velocity (P–V) parameters within the designated process window of P–V space. By doing so, it is possible to significantly optimize the fatigue performance of as-built Ti-6Al-4V alloy components. A particularly notable feature of this work was the application of Basquin’s Law in the fitting of the S–N curve data corresponding to both printing and process parameters. While the Basquin stress–life relation fit the LoF and surface roughness process parameters, it was a very poor fit for the keyhole porosity, indicating that keyhole porosity plays a very small role in optimized LPBF-fabricated Ti-6Al-4V components where surface roughness dominates. This is an important feature that encompasses over 1 years of fatigue performance data as it applies to complex components of L-PBF-fabricated Ti-6Al-4V alloy, in particular.

Fatigue behaviour of laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF) Ti–6Al–4V, Al–Si–Mg and stainless steels: a brief overview Fatigue and crack growth characteristics are essential cyclic properties of additively manufactured (AM) components for load-bearing applications, which are less reported in the literature than static properties. The fatigue behaviour of AM components is more complicated than those produced by conventional fabrication techniques (casting and forging) because of the multiplicity of different influencing factors like defect distribution, inhomogeneity of the microstructure and consequent anisotropy. Therefore, it is crucial to understand fatigue performance under different loading conditions to enhance AM application in aerospace, automotive, and other industries. The present work summarises the published literature for fatigue properties of popular metals (Ti–6Al–4V, Al–Si–Mg and stainless steels) produced by the laser powder-bed-fusion (L-PBF) process. Moreover, process parameters, post-processing treatments and microstructures of these alloys are discussed to evaluate the current state-of-the-art of fatigue and crack growth properties of L-PBF metals. The static properties of these alloys are also included to incorporate only those cases for which fatigue behaviour are discussed later in this review to make a correlation between the static and fatigue properties for these alloys. The effects of build orientation, microstructure, heat treatment, surface roughness and defects on fatigue strength and fatigue crack growth threshold are observed and critically analysed based on available literature.

This study also highlights the common and contrary findings in the literature associated with various influential factors to comprehensively understand the cyclic loading behaviour of L-PBF produced metal alloys. Additive manufacturing (AM) is a new and emerging technique that has the potential to produce complex and customised high strength products directly from three-dimensional CAD models. AM is primarily a layer by layer process, which the ASTM has defined as ‘‘a process of joining materials to make objects from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtract manufacturing methodologies. Synonyms: additive fabrication, additive processes, additive techniques, additive layer manufacturing, and freedom fabrication’’ ( 2 ). This technology was introduced in the early 1 s in Japan by Kodama (Kodama 1 ), who invented the 3D polymerisation process, which is known today as stereolithography. Almost ten years later, E. ( 1 ) successfully printed a 3D building with all its attributes using their novel process. Since then, machines have been improved continuously and employed to develop products of enhanced functionality and often great complexity.

Almost ten years later, E. ( 1 ) successfully printed a 3D building with all its attributes using their novel process. Since then, machines have been improved continuously and employed to develop products of enhanced functionality and often great complexity. Today an increasing number of manufacturing companies use AM because it can dramatically shorten fabrication time and cost due to the elimination of production and assembly steps and also reduce the material waste and environmental impact compared to conventional processing. Although AM metals have benefits over casting, the AM technologies are still associated with the inevitable presence of processing defects such as pores, lack of fusion pores, cracks and impurities, and can lead to a build-up of residual stresses ( 2 ). Several other factors, such as raw material characteristics, chemical composition, powder density distribution, process parameters, and post-processing, affect the resultant part properties ( 2 ). Furthermore, additive manufacturing (Appleyard 2 ) transforms powder materials into solid geometries (components) at high solidification rates, which affects the microstructure of the deposited material and hence the mechanical properties. Although extensive research effort has been directed to characterise the AM parameters, the uncertainty of the load-bearing properties of 3D printed structures is still a challenge, yet to be fully addressed for different AM processes (Frazier 2 ; 2 ).

Although extensive research effort has been directed to characterise the AM parameters, the uncertainty of the load-bearing properties of 3D printed structures is still a challenge, yet to be fully addressed for different AM processes (Frazier 2 ; 2 ). Various AM techniques are available depending upon the material to be fabricated and the mechanism of layer formations. However, this review only focuses on the metal alloys produced by Laser Powder Bed Fusion (L-PBF) (Frazier 2 ), often also called Selective Laser Melting process (SLM) (SLM Solutions GmbH). L-PBF produces complex-shaped components with several structural alloys to meet the requirements in different fields, such as aerospace, automotive, and biomedical applications ( 2 ). With the advancement of AM techniques, many researchers have paid attention to understanding the effects of different factors on the mechanical and microstructural properties of AM materials. The majority of the work has focused on the static mechanical (tensile/compression) properties of the popular AM alloy, Ti–6Al–4V alloy ( 2 ; de 2 ; 2 ; 2 ; 2 ; 2 a; 2 ). Only a few studies have concerned with other AM alloys, such as AlSi Mg ( 2 ; 2 ; 2 ) and stainless steel ( 2 b; 2 ).

In general, most AM materials and processes outperform their conventional counterparts in terms of static strength ( 2 ; 2 ). This is because of the finer microstructure from L-PBF generated by the rapid melting and cooling process ( 2 ). However, porosity and defects are often unavoidable features of bulk AM fabricated parts. The presence of pores does not reduce the stiffness or the yield strength ( 2 ). However, it can act as a crack initiator under cyclic loading, thus reduce the fatigue strength by about 4 % ( 2 ; 2 ). Furthermore, fatigue failure of AM metals is more complicated than conventional fabrication because of the involvement of different influencing factors, for example, the inhomogeneous microstructure and varying pore size and orientation with the change in process parameters ( 2 ). The presence of various AM defects and microstructural inhomogeneities is affected by the processing parameters. Furthermore, the presence of residual stress and surface roughness may cause degradation in material properties.

Furthermore, the presence of residual stress and surface roughness may cause degradation in material properties. Therefore, this review aims to interpret the change in mechanical properties for different AM parameters and postprocessing treatments. The static failure mechanisms are always a fundamental matter of interest, and therefore, has been reviewed ( 2 ; 2 ; Herderick 2 ; 2 ; 2 ) by a number of researchers for AM alloys. Therefore, this work will focus more on fatigue and fatigue fracture behaviours of AM produced alloys, particularly for the L-PBF process. In recent years, the fatigue and fatigue failure behaviour of AM alloys have become a focal point ( 2 ; 2 ; 2 ; 2 ). To date, there have been some reports on the fatigue properties of Ti–6Al–4V ( 2 ; Frazier 2 ; 2 ; 2 ; 2 ; 2 ; 2 ), but fewer on Al–Si–Mg ( 2 ; 2 ) and steels ( 2 ).

In a review, ( 2 ) have reported the overall static and fatigue strength of AM materials while ( 2 ) and ( 2 ) summarized the quantitative understanding of the fatigue behaviour in particular for Ti–6Al– 4V alloy. (2 ) provided an overview of the fatigue characteristics of AM materials. Other articles ( 2 ; 2 ; D. 2 a, b, c) have studied the state-of-the-art Al alloys produced via L-PBF processes, considering the effect of various factors on the microstructure and mechanical behaviour. The limited data available for fatigue and fracture toughness of AM produced metals leads to uncertainty in the durability assessment of AM parts. Therefore, it is imperative to have a thorough understanding of the fatigue behaviour of additively manufactured alloys to improve the reliability of AM fabricated parts. In this context, this review aims to focus on the currently available fatigue, and fatigue crack growth data of L-PBF produced common alloys, namely titanium (Ti–6Al–4V), aluminium (Al–Si–Mg) and stainless steels (3 L, 1 -PH), to draw up a state-ofthe-art portrait of the present literature.

The basic principle of an AM system is to generate a 3D computer CAD model of a component and then print it using a layer by layer process by melting powder or wire feedstock. There are many AM technologies available commercially which have their advantages and disadvantages associated with the intended application. In this section, AM technologies, process parameters and microstructure will be discussed to correlate the effect of these factors on the fatigue of various metal alloys ( 2 ; Frazier 2 ; 2 ). 2.1 Types of additive manufacturing Additive manufacturing processes can be categorised in several ways depending upon the material to be fabricated and the mechanism in which the layers are developed. Frazier (Frazier 2 ) has divided the AM system into three board categories as (i) powder bed systems, (ii) powder feed systems and, (iii) wire feed systems.

Frazier (Frazier 2 ) has divided the AM system into three board categories as (i) powder bed systems, (ii) powder feed systems and, (iii) wire feed systems. Powder bed fusion (PBF) system can fabricate complex parts with a higher degree of freedom by directly melting powder metal ( 2 ) and has gained popularity in producing light-weight parts ( 2 b) and functionally graded material ( 2 a). PBF has three steps: uniform deposition of powder on the bedplate from a hopper or a powder reservoir to create a thin layer, the exposure of an energy source onto the bed to locally melt the powder, and finally lowering the build plate to rake another layer of powder when the first layer is completely solidified. These steps continue until the completion of designed metal parts (Van der 1 ). Based on the energy source used, the powder bed fusion system is further be classified into two categories: Laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF) and Electron beam powder bed fusion (E-PBF).

Based on the energy source used, the powder bed fusion system is further be classified into two categories: Laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF) and Electron beam powder bed fusion (E-PBF). Both L-PBF (EOS Electro Optical Systems GmbH,; Trumpf GmbH) and E-PBF have the same basic operational principles; however, E-PBF uses an electron beam ( 2 , 2 a) as the energy source instead of a laser beam (Concept Laser GmbH) as used in L-PBF. L-PBF requires an inert gas atmosphere to ensure that the residual oxygen content is no more than 0.1% ( 2 ). Nitrogen or argon is fed into the chamber to protect the metal powder from oxidation and to carry away any byproduct spatter from the main part ( 2 ). On the contrary, for the E-PBF process, the operational environment is a vacuum (\1 Pa) to avoid any electron charging as well as to enhance the heat conduction process ( 2 a). In contrast to PBF system, powder feed systems or laser direct energy deposition (L-DED) systems use nozzles to feed the powder into the work surface. L-DED enables large volume scale production at a higher built rate.

process ( 2 a). In contrast to PBF system, powder feed systems or laser direct energy deposition (L-DED) systems use nozzles to feed the powder into the work surface. L-DED enables large volume scale production at a higher built rate. Two different systems of L-DED are possible, one in which the work part is stationary while the deposition head moves for each layer of the build. In the other type, the deposition head remains stationary with a moving workpiece (Frazier 2 ). Wire feed system, also known as Laser beam freeform fabrication, is a modified version of the powder feed system in which the wire is used as a feedstock instead of powder (Frazier 2 ). The energy source for this process can be an electron beam, laser beam or plasma arc. The wire feed process is suited to a large build volume; however, it requires more rigorous and careful post-processing than PBF or DED. Among all these AM technologies, L-PBF is popular with the automotive and aerospace industry; therefore, it will focus on this review. AM process parameters Both powder properties and machine parameters influence the microstructure and mechanical properties of AM parts (Das 2 ; 2 ; 2 ). In metal powder-based processes, the powder morphology, particle size, chemical composition and thermal conductivity are crucial to the success of the AM process. Moreover, to achieve high-density bulk material, good flowability of particles is required, which maintains homogeneity while spreading over the bed. Spherical powder leads to better packing density than irregular powder particles, as the flow time is reduced ( 2 ; Olakanmi 2 ). The machine parameters such as laser power, scan speed, hatch spacing, and layer thickness significantly influence the final AM part. These parameters contribute directly to the part density (Gu 2 ), phase stability, anisotropy and microstructure ( 2 ; 2 ). By varying process parameters, an optimal energy range can be found relative to the machines and alloys to be printed ( 2 ; 2 ; 2 ). The laser scanning strategy (scan vector length and rotation) is a geometrical pattern that the beam follows during the powder melting process. The commonly adopted strategies are single melt (with different scan rotation) and chessboard, .

The commonly adopted strategies are single melt (with different scan rotation) and chessboard, . Scanning strategy may affect the residual stress generation, porosity formation, anisotropy behaviour, and mechanical properties ( 2 b; 2 ; 2 ; 2 ). Build orientation of parts in the build plate affects the microstructural direction, defects and consequently, mechanical properties ( 2 ; 2 ; 2 ). Build platform heating is another crucial parameter for the L-PBF process. Pre-heating of the platform reduces the thermal gradient between the layers, thus providing a relatively low cooling rate. Therefore, elongated dendrites can be observed for pre-heated samples as it allows more time in the solidification process ( 2 ). Pre-heating also reduces residual stress and cracking ( 2 ) The machine parameters used to produce the fatigue samples discussed in this study are listed in. In AM, the material undergoes a complex thermal cycle which includes a large number of rapid melting and subsequent solidification processes. The microstructure of AM processed material is, therefore, a result of this thermal process, which is more complex to analyse in comparison with cast material ( 2 a, b).

One of the fundamental challenges of AM is the generation of various inhomogeneities and defects during the fabrication process ( 2 ; 2 ; 2 ; 2 ; 2 ; Zhang 2 ; 2 ).

in Sects. 3 and 4. One of the fundamental challenges of AM is the generation of various inhomogeneities and defects during the fabrication process ( 2 ; 2 ; 2 ; 2 ; 2 ; Zhang 2 ; 2 ). Almost all AM processes produce internal defects that may be acceptable but within a tolerable limit based on the product’s mechanical performance requirement. The most commonly observed AM defect is bulk porosity ( 2 ; Frazier 2 ; 2 ). The formation of AM porosity is strongly affected by variations in the process parameters ( 2 ), and can occur from a number of sources ( 2 ), . Both powder properties ( 2 ) and machine parameters ( 2 ; 2 ; 2 ) influence the formation of pores, their size and density. Powder properties, e.g., size, density and composition, are other fundamental factors controlling the defects of AM metal ( 2 ; 2 ; 2 ). Contaminations of moisture, gases and organics in the powder hinders the densification process and leads to pore formations ( 2 ). Metallurgical pores also referred to as gas pores, ( 2 ) are usually spherical in shape and less than 1 lm in size ( 2 ; 2 ).

hinders the densification process and leads to pore formations ( 2 ). Metallurgical pores also referred to as gas pores, ( 2 ) are usually spherical in shape and less than 1 lm in size ( 2 ; 2 ). Gas pores form due to the presence of moisture on the surface of the powder feedstock leading to H absorption in the melt ( 2 a, b; 2 ). The irregular shaped pores found in AM parts can be identified as key-hole or Lack of fusion pores (LOF), formation of which is more controlled by energy density input ( 2 ). A very high energy density (exceeding boiling point) within the melt pool leads to material evaporation. The evaporation of material generates evaporation pressure that forms deep depression on the part known as key-hole pores ( 2 ). A fast scan speed associated with low heat input forms more irregular and elongated pores ( 2 ; 2 ; 2 ) in AM parts due to insufficient heating of the previous layer, known as LOF pores ( 2 ; 2 ). However, high energy input is claimed to reduce overall porosity density in many studies ( 2 ; 2 ; 2 ).

AM parts due to insufficient heating of the previous layer, known as LOF pores ( 2 ; 2 ). However, high energy input is claimed to reduce overall porosity density in many studies ( 2 ; 2 ; 2 ). Two types of LOF pores is found in AM parts: inadequate re-melting between the layers during the solidification process and incomplete (partial) melting of powders, . For the latter case, LOF defects contain un-melted powder particles. LOF pores are mostly found near the melt pool boundaries or in-between scanning layers. In addition to energy input, an adequate overlapping of laser tracks between layers and preheating of the bedplate also reduce the density of LOF porosity ( 2 ) as well as hydrogen porosity formation ( 2 ), by improving the wettability of the melt pool Preheating of the base plate also reduces the residual stress within the part, along with susceptibility to crack formation by reducing the thermal gradient ( 2 ; 2 ). Interlayer defects may also form due to the presence of pregenerated defects on the previous layer, which reduces the flowability of the molten metal and thus leads to weak bonding between the layers.

thermal gradient ( 2 ; 2 ). Interlayer defects may also form due to the presence of pregenerated defects on the previous layer, which reduces the flowability of the molten metal and thus leads to weak bonding between the layers. Besides, insufficient inert gas flow during the L-PBF process may cause trapping of the by-products, which in turns increases the lack of fusion defects. Moreover, inert gas itself can be a reason for the formation of gas pores if nonuniform recoil pressure occurs within the melt pool surface ( 2 ). The scanning strategy also controls the solidification process and affects the defect formation and location. The unidirectional or zigzag scanning process produces more LOF pores between the layers (Bauereiß et al. 2 ; 2 b), whilst, chessboard or island scan strategy is the most successful in reducing repetitive defect accumulation in the same location ( 2 ). Both gas and LOF pores in AM parts can be detrimental for mechanical properties such as fracture toughness and fatigue strength, particularly if pores are found to be highly concentrated in one place or near the surface ( 2 ; 2 ). Surface roughness is another crucial defect of AM parts, which is detrimental to fatigue performance ( 2 ; 2 ).

if pores are found to be highly concentrated in one place or near the surface ( 2 ; 2 ). Surface roughness is another crucial defect of AM parts, which is detrimental to fatigue performance ( 2 ; 2 ). Compared to conventional manufacturing, AM causes higher surface roughness in the as-built condition due to partially-melted particles adhering to the surface ( 2 ). The surface defects or features are highly affected by build orientation ( 2 ; 2 ), build rate (Frazier 2 ) and energy deposition and scanning strategy ( 2 ). An increase in build rate causes reduced surface quality ( 2 ). Moreover, in the L-PBF method, while printing the part in an inclined position, the downward-facing side produces more surface irregularities ( c) due to thermal edge effects ( 2 ). Laser remelting has been proposed to decrease surface roughness in steel ( 2 ). From the above discussion, it is evident that the process parameters affect defect formation in AM. For example, a reduction in laser power may produce less gas porosity. However, it may lead to the generation of more LOF pores. Therefore, optimization of process parameters based on their interrelationships as well as the future application of the parts may help to reduce defect accumulation and improve mechanical performance.

Build orientation is the other factor that causes variation in static properties of L-PBF alloys. In most cases, the horizontally built samples show higher static strength than vertical or orthogonal samples in asfabricated conditions. This is due to the microstructure formation and grain orientation ( 2 ). grain orientation in vertically built samples promotes dislocation movement and consequently lowers yield strength. The anisotropy in tensile strength is due to the presence of defects since defects cause early failure ( 2 ). The difference in elongation property is partially due to LOF pores in between layers, which causes the vertical samples to fail early by expanding the pores along the loading direction ( 2 ). However, the durability of AM products in various load-bearing engineering applications involving cyclic loading is still not well understood due to the scarcity of fatigue and fracture toughness data. In general, the fatigue strength of as-built AM alloys is low due to the inherent defects, the heterogeneous microstructure and surface roughness of AM metal parts. It could be expected that the remnant porosity within the AM part does not change the yield strength much as long as the pore size is below a critical value. However, this may not be the case for fatigue properties. Besides, parts having high ductility may provide increased fatigue life by decreasing crack propagation rate through the grains. However, crack initiation at lower cycles can also be from internal pores instead of surface pores if the volume of internal porosity is very high, which reduces crack nucleation time ( 1 ). Crack initiation primarily depends on crack size at low-stress levels, however, at high-stress levels, surface defects dominate as the stress around these leads to exceeding the crack growth threshold value (DKth) ( Chandran 2 ). Therefore, to improve the fatigue life of L-PBF Ti–6Al–4V alloy it is important to study crack growth properties as crack initiation has a negligible impact on the fatigue life. Build orientation causes anisotropy in the mechanical property of AM materials ( 2 ). This level of anisotropy is higher for as-built samples ( 2 ), and the post-processing techniques like HIP considerably reduce this ( 2 ). AM processed Al materials can outperform conventionally fabricated Al alloys in terms of static strength ( 2 ; 2 ); however, the inherent porosity and surface imperfections of AM parts are detrimental to fatigue performance. In order to ensure the quality and integrity of the AM structural parts, a rigorous mechanical investigation is often warranted, which could be costly and time-consuming.

The literature data concerning L-PBF Al alloys’ fatigue performance is very limited so far, which are listed in. It is important to note that the reported fatigue properties of L-PBF Al alloy are lower than that obtained by the conventional processing in as-built condition. This low fatigue life in the as-built state is attributed to the high surface roughness of AM parts due to unmelted powder at the periphery ( 2 ; 2 ). The surface roughness of as-built AM parts leads to rapid crack initiation and propagation as surface defects impart higher stress compared to internal defects (Murakami 2 ). Cracks for as-built samples can also propagate from sub-surface pores due to the presence of residual stress associated with the L-PBF process. Machining or polishing is recommended to improve ( 2 a; 2 ; 2 ; 2 ) fatigue strength as compared to as-built samples by reducing roughness ( 2 ). However, the improvement in fatigue life after machining is not consistent among various work in literature.

The magnitude of surface roughness of as-built parts depends on scanning strategy as well, such as contouring. Contouring is a scanning strategy where a boundary scan (outer circle for round fatigue sample) is performed, followed by inner hatch filling for solid material. Samples produced without contour (boundary scanning) show less average roughness, and provide similar fatigue strength as milled samples ( 2 ).

Steel is one of the most widely used engineering materials owing to its high strength stiffness, and cost effectiveness leading to a wide variety of applications. Several types of steel can be successfully processed by L-PBF ( 2 ; 2 ), such as maraging steel ( 2 ), 1 -PH ( 2 b; 2 ), AISI 3 ( 2 ; 2 ; 2 ), and 1 PH ( 2 ). About 3 % of published AM metal alloy work has been undertaken for steel. A wide range of research has been carried out concerning the processes, microstructure evolution and static loading properties ( 2 ; 2 ; 2 b; 2 b; 2 ) of AM steel parts. Fatigue behaviour of the steel is governed by defects and microstructure properties which can be tailored through various heat treatments. However, the sensitivity of the fatigue behaviour is not consistent with all types of heat treatment, and it can vary for regimes as well. Stress relief treatment does not significantly improve overall fatigue life (crack initiation ? propagation), although it delays crack initiation by removing internal residual stress ( 2 ). Fatigue crack initiation is mostly from grain boundary defects. Annealing changes grain size and removes significant defects on boundaries, thus shifts the fracture tendency from grain defects to pore defects remaining after annealing ( 2 b). This procedure provides a better fatigue life. Heat treatment has been reported to improve the fatigue life of L-PBF 3 L steel in the HCF regime and lower the fatigue life for LCF ( 2 ; 2 b).

2 b). This procedure provides a better fatigue life. Heat treatment has been reported to improve the fatigue life of L-PBF 3 L steel in the HCF regime and lower the fatigue life for LCF ( 2 ; 2 b). The reduction in LCF is coarsening grain size through the Hall Petch ( 2 ) effect as fatigue life of this alloy mainly depends on monotonic strength than pores ( 2 ). At very high cycle regimes, internal pores cause crack initiation; therefore, heat treatment increases fatigue life by removing pores. The opposite behaviour has been found for 1 -PH steel, where annealing is beneficial for LCF and unfavourable for HCF ( 2 ). Heat treatment of 1 -PH steel causes precipitation strengthening that leads to the formation of more defects as well as low HCF properties. However, this strengthening mechanism improves tensile and LCF properties ( 2 ; 2 ; 2 ). These findings reveal that both grain size and defects can affect the fatigue life of steel, in ways different to the other alloys. Therefore, the choice of heat treatment on steel fatigue must be application dependent.

As seen in this section, most of the work pertaining to L-PBF produced steel is for 3 L alloy. This alloy provides excellent tensile and fatigue properties, even in the as-built condition. Like Al-alloy, the effect of heat treatment on the fatigue life of steels is not very straightforward. Similar heat treatment causes different microstructure formation among various steel type depending on alloy composition. Therefore, the choice of heat treatment needs to be customised for the particular type of steel and expected application requirements.

The Effect of Laser Powder Bed Fusion Process on Ti-6Al-4V Powder Abstract: Additive manufacturing with Ti-6Al-4V laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF) processes offers significant advantages for producing high-performance components. Hence, powder reuse strategies are crucial for reducing manufacturing costs and environmental impact, and reliable part properties. This study investigates the influence of L-PBF processing on the properties of virgin, recycled, and spatter Ti-6Al-4V powders. Particle size distribution, morphology, microstructure, chemical composition, and microhardness were determined using a laser particle size analyzer, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and microhardness testing. Virgin powder exhibited a narrow size distribution and spherical morphology. Recycled powder showed a slightly broader distribution and increased irregularity, while spatter powder had a significantly broader distribution with large, irregular particles. Microstructural analysis revealed that all powders exhibited an acicular a¢ martensite microstructure, while the presence of twins was unique to the virgin powders. The average a¢ lath thickness was similar for virgin and spatter powders, with a slightly higher in the recycled powders. XRD confirmed the presence of the a¢ phase, and dislocation density was highest in virgin powder and lowest in spatter powder.

average a¢ lath thickness was similar for virgin and spatter powders, with a slightly higher in the recycled powders. XRD confirmed the presence of the a¢ phase, and dislocation density was highest in virgin powder and lowest in spatter powder. Microhardness measurements indicated a slight decrease in hardness for recycled powders and a wider range of hardness values for spatter powders compared to virgin powders Additive manufacturing (AM), also known as 3D printing, has revolutionized the fabrication of complex geometries and customized components across diverse industries, including aerospace, medical, and automotive.1–4 Among the various AM techniques, powder bed fusion (PBF) processes, such as laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF), have emerged as frontrunners due to their ability to produce highperformance metal parts with intricate details and exceptional mechanical properties.

medical, and automotive.1–4 Among the various AM techniques, powder bed fusion (PBF) processes, such as laser powder bed fusion (L-PBF), have emerged as frontrunners due to their ability to produce highperformance metal parts with intricate details and exceptional mechanical properties. L-PBF employs a high-powered laser to selectively melt and fuse powders layer by layer, typically operating at lower ambient powder bed temperatures not higher than 2 C. While the cost of metal powders, such as gasatomized Ti-6Al-4V, has decreased recently, reaching approximately 7 .6 euros per kilogram,5 the material cost remains a major contributor to the overall cost of producing a cubic centimeter of titanium part.6 Therefore, implementing powder reuse strategies is crucial for minimizing environmental impact, reducing waste generation and manufacturing costs, aligning with sustainable manufacturing practices.7,8 In a review article on the subject, 9 compared commonly used metallic powder reuse methods (single batch and collective ageing, top-up, and refreshing) focusing on powder traceability and handling procedures. However, repeated exposure to the L-PBF process can alter the powder characteristics, potentially affecting the morphology, size distribution, flowability, and packing density.

powder reuse methods (single batch and collective ageing, top-up, and refreshing) focusing on powder traceability and handling procedures. However, repeated exposure to the L-PBF process can alter the powder characteristics, potentially affecting the morphology, size distribution, flowability, and packing density. Ultimately, the quality of the final L-PBF parts, specifically their microstructural integrity and mechanical performance, is intrinsically linked to the initial properties of the Ti-6Al-4V powders.1 Therefore, a comprehensive understanding of how L-PBF processes change the properties of Ti-6Al-4V powders is crucial for optimizing manufacturing outcomes and ensuring the reliability of the fabricated components. Powders employed in PBF can be broadly classified into three categories: virgin, recycled, and spatter.1 Virgin powders are unused and represent the initial feedstock material. Recycled powders have been recovered from previous PBF builds without direct exposure to the laser. Recycled powder may exhibit altered properties due to exposure to elevated temperatures and the build environment. Spatter powders have been ejected and resolidified during laser melting, often exhibiting irregular shapes and surface features. The changes induced by L-PBF processing vary depending on the powder type and the number of reuse cycles.

temperatures and the build environment. Spatter powders have been ejected and resolidified during laser melting, often exhibiting irregular shapes and surface features. The changes induced by L-PBF processing vary depending on the powder type and the number of reuse cycles. Virgin powder is generally considered to possess optimal characteristics for L-PBF, while recycled and spatter powders may exhibit altered properties due to exposure to heat, laser radiation, and mechanical interactions during the process.1 To determine the essential powder characteristics for PBF systems and investigate the impact of recycling on these properties, 1 conducted a series of experiments. The results highlighted the important relationship between the thermal history and microstructural changes in influencing the performance of recycled metal powders in additive manufacturing. Similarly, 1 summarized the findings of studies examining the impact of powder characteristics, including morphology, chemistry, and microstructure, on final parts in PBF systems and the influence of PBF manufacturing processes on powder characteristics. Recognizing the significance of powder characterization, ASTM developed the F standard,1 which defines the methods for measuring particle size, morphology, chemical composition, flowability, and density for powders used in PBF systems. Employing these methods, 1 investigated the chemical composition and particle size distribution of Ti-6Al-4V powders following PBF exposure.

F standard,1 which defines the methods for measuring particle size, morphology, chemical composition, flowability, and density for powders used in PBF systems. Employing these methods, 1 investigated the chemical composition and particle size distribution of Ti-6Al-4V powders following PBF exposure. They particle size decreased with reuse cycles, while the chemical composition of the reused powder remained within the ASTM F specification.1 7 examined the influence of powder reuse strategies on morphology and composition in PBF manufacturing for Inconel 7 , Ti-6Al-4V, AlSi Mg, and Scammalloy powders. AlSi Mg exhibited the most significant variation between virgin and used powders, followed by Inconel 7 , while Ti Al V and Scammalloy demonstrated the least impact of reuse. To guide end-users in determining whether continued powder reuse is advisable, the study presents a decision diagram based on the proposed characterization methods. While these studies provide valuable insights into the effects of PBF on powder behavior, a comprehensive understanding of the process’s impact on microstructural characteristics, such as phase thickness, lattice parameters, and dislocation density, remains elusive. This knowledge gap is particularly critical for optimizing powder reuse strategies, ensuring part quality and expanding the applicability of PBF AM. This study aims to conduct a systematic investigation of the effects exerted by L-PBF processing on the properties of Ti-6Al-4V powders.

This study aims to conduct a systematic investigation of the effects exerted by L-PBF processing on the properties of Ti-6Al-4V powders. Our investigation focuses on three distinct powder groups: virgin (unused), recycled, and spatter. By analyzing the characteristics of the powders before and after their exposure to L-PBF, we aim to elucidate the specific changes induced by the process.
